Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
1
Scope and Framework
1.1
Scope
This GSOP defines the quality “Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls”.
It covers specific requirements associated with manufacturing processes, particularly with regard to 
the following topics:
-
Personnel
-
Premises and equipment
-
Materials handling
-
Process and methods
-
Manufacturing Documentation
It applies cross-functionally to all organizations, processes and products within the GMP/GDP and ISO 
regulated areas of Product Supply and related interfaces, e.g., product development.
1.2
Framework 
This GSOP represents one core requirement document of the process group “Supply Product”. 
The overarching framework is provided in the General Process Document “Supply Product” (GSOP 
1747).
This GSOP has interfaces with:
-
GSOP 242 “Requirements for Cleaning of Equipment in the Manufacture of APIs and Drug 
Products”
-
GSOP 136 “Requirements for Stability Study”
This GSOP is complemented by:
-
GSOP 268 “Quality Requirements for Clean Rooms”
-
GOI 1496 “Requirements for Container Closure Integrity”
-
GOI 1040 “Requirements for Prevention of Cross-Contamination in Shared Facilities”

3
Definitions
For generic definitions see SUP 200 “Glossary”.
Batch 
A defined quantity of starting material, packaging material, or product 
processed in a single process or series of processes so that it is expected 
to be homogeneous. It may sometimes be necessary to divide a batch into 
a number of sub-batches, which may later brought together to form a final 
homogeneous batch. In the case of terminal sterilization, the sub-batch 
(syn. autoclave lot) size is determined by the capacity of the autoclave. In 
continuous manufacture, the batch must correspond to a defined fraction 
of the production, characterized by its intended homogeneity. The batch 
size can be defined either as a fixed quantity or as the amount produced in 
a fixed time interval.
Batch number
A distinctive combination of numbers and/or letters which uniquely 
identifies a batch on the labels, its batch records, and corresponding 
certificates of analysis, etc.

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Blending
Process of combining materials within the same specification to produce a 
homogeneous intermediate or API (with respect to API manufacture)
Device 
History 
Record
All documents associated with the manufacture of a medical device. They 
provide a history of each device and of all circumstances pertinent to the 
quality of the device.
Device 
Master 
Record
A compilation of records containing the procedures and specifications for
a finished device.
Executed 
Batch 
Record
The documentation of the implementation of manufacturing processes for 
a batch. This is the batch record with actual production data and contains 
the documentation of all facts concerning the actual manufacturing 
processes which concern the quality of the API or drug product.
In-Process Control
Checks performed during production in order to monitor the output and to 
control the performance of those manufacturing processes or steps that 
may cause variability in the characteristics of in-process materials and, if 
necessary, to adjust the process to ensure that the product conforms to its 
specifications.
Physical barrier
At least one or a combination of the following: Closed cupboards, steel 
cases, separate closed rooms, fenced areas.
Manufacturing 
Description
The manufacturing description (equivalent to the corresponding Technical 
Registration Documents) is a condensed form of the Master Batch 
Record issued for submission purposes to regulatory authorities.
Where required, a more detailed manufacturing description or a complete 
Master Batch Record might also be submitted.
Restricted access
A controlled mechanical key and/or electronic key, key-card or key-code is 
necessary to enter the specific area.
4
Purpose & Set-Up
4.1
Purpose of SOP
The proper performance of manufacturing processes and the accompanying in-process controls are 
an important aspect of the GMP/QSR1 Quality System for Drug Products incl. Biologics, Cell and Gene 
Therapy Products and Investigational Medicinal Products (IMPs), Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients 
(APIs & API intermediates) and Medical Devices.
4.2
General Guidance for the SOP
All manufacturing processes and production operations follow clearly defined procedures. They 
comply with the principles of Good Manufacturing Practice (or other quality management systems) to 
consistently obtain Drug Products, APIs, Medical Devices, Cosmetics and Food Supplements of the 
requisite quality and to be in accordance with the relevant specifications and marketing 
authorizations/clinical trial authorizations and registrations (for Medical Devices).
1 Note: Medical Devices are regulated by applicable standards (e.g., ISO 13485) and/or legal requirements
(e.g., 21 CFR part 820 Quality System Regulations). However, for simplification purposes, the term „GMP“ is 
used instead of “GMP/QSR/ISO13485” throughout this GSOP.

INTERNAL
4.3
Roles and Responsibilities
Local responsible 
person for PsT
Includes local legal obligations and laws once the present requirements 
are locally implemented.
Surveys local implementation and advice the local management and staff 
in taking measure regarding PsT.
Ensures maintenance of the marker “pilferable” in SAP for all PsT; 
alternatively, if SAP is not available an appropriate maker to indicate all 
PsT is used in the locally available IT system. 
Acts as first local point of contact with respect to PsT related rules.
Collects information about irregularities in his/her area of responsibility 
and shares these with the person locally responsible for complaint 
handling to ensure reporting in the corresponding complaint management 
system. 
Production 
Personnel (e.g., 
Head of Production) 
Ensures that products are produced and stored under GMP and 
according to the appropriate documentation to obtain the required quality 
[17].
Ensures that manufacturing description and master batch records/Device 
Master Record are in compliance with the GMP requirements of the local 
Regulatory Authority, Technical Documentation/Design Dossier and with 
the specifications in the marketing authorization.
Approves master/DMR and executed batch records/DHR and associated 
records.
Ensures monitoring and control of the manufacturing environment (e.g., 
particle and microbiology monitoring).
Ensures the complete documentation of the manufacturing of each batch 
including the documentation and investigations of deviations and changes 
which occurred. 
Ensures In-Process Controls are sampled, performed, and evaluated 
(e.g., bioburden) in case the task has been delegated by the accountable 
QC department.
Ensures that the executed batch records or Device History Records are 
evaluated and signed by a responsible person for production before they 
are sent to the Quality Unit. 
Ensures that an adequate number of trained personnel with the 
necessary qualification and practical experience is available for all 
manufacturing and testing-relevant activities 
Quality Assurance 
Unit initially 
releasing the 
product (QA)
Owns quality related batch data of the released batch.
Quality Control
Ensures In-Process Controls are sampled, performed, and evaluated 
(e.g., bioburden).
Delegates sampling, performance, and evaluation of IPCs to the 
production or warehouse personnel as required.

5
Detailed description (Requirements and Procedures)
5.1
Personnel
Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
5.1.1
Personnel

Monitor compliance of GMP requirements (as shared responsibility with 
Head of QA).

Ensure that an adequate number of personnel with the necessary 
qualification and practical experience is available for all manufacturing and 
testing-relevant activities.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls
Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls
Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

__data__.text:

Ensure that an adequate number of personnel with the necessary 
qualification and practical experience is available for all manufacturing and 
testing-relevant activities. 

Ensure that responsibilities placed on any one individual is not so extensive 
as to present any risk to quality. 

Record specific duties of people in responsible positions in written job 
descriptions:

Provide adequate authority and capacity to carry out their 
responsibilities.

Document responsibilities and liabilities of personnel and ensure that these 
are known and understood by the personnel.

Instruct and supervise personnel appropriately (including employees who 
temporarily work in production areas, laboratories, or warehouses such as 
personnel assigned by service providers) by authorized people:

Follow for further guidance the instructions of GSOP 192 “Manage 
GMP/GDP Training”.

Preferably visitors or untrained personnel not taken into production and 
quality control areas:

Accompany in case it is not unavoidable, by trained and authorized 
personnel. 

Provide information in advance particularly about personal hygiene and 
the prescribed protective clothing. 

Follow rigorous and clearly defined decontamination procedures for entering 
sterile-product areas especially for personnel who have been engaged in 
the processing and/or testing of tissue materials or of cultures of (live) 
microorganisms other than those used in the current manufacturing process 
[1]:

Establish for e.g., QC microbiology staff doing environmental 
monitoring, an appropriate washing and disinfection process.

See also provisions according to GOI 1040 “Requirements for 
Prevention of Cross-Contamination in Shared Facilities”.

Follow for further guidance GSOP 268 “Quality requirements for Clean 
Rooms”, and for Medical Devices GOI 1138 “Requirements for Medical 
Devices – Site, Facilities, and Equipment Control”.
Head of Production

5.2
Premises and Equipment
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference
5.2.1
Premises

Design buildings and facilities suitably:

A separate weighing room for the weighing of starting materials used 
in the production of drug products is required e.g., due to
a)
Separation of storage and manufacturing items and spaces
b)
Clear flow of materials and personnel
c)
Prevention of cross-contamination

Identify production materials, containers, major items of equipment and – 
where appropriate – rooms and points-of-use of utilities, e.g., by means of 
a manual or computerized (coding) identification system: 

Indicate – where applicable – the product or material being processed 
[2], it´s strength, batch number, the stage of production, and its status 
(or reference to the status, e.g., quarantined, approved, rejected [3]).
Remarks:

Where no separate weighing rooms are available, ensure the prevention of 
cross-contamination by organizational measures.

__data__.text:
Remarks:

Where no separate weighing rooms are available, ensure the prevention of 
cross-contamination by organizational measures.
Production Personnel
5.2.2
Equipment

Design, construct, place, install and qualify all GMP-relevant equipment 
(for Medical Devices: critical equipment) used in the manufacturing 
process appropriately to facilitate maintenance, adjustment, cleaning, and 
use:  

Qualify all inspection, measuring, weighing, recording, and control 
equipment, including mechanical, automated, or electronical inspection 
and test equipment, to ensure that they are suitable for the intended 
purposes according to GSOP 125 “Commission and Qualify Technical 
Systems”.  

Follow the instructions of GSOP 428 “Manage Maintenance” and GSOP 
533 “Manage Calibration” to ensure that equipment is routinely maintained 
and calibrated. 

Establish measures to indicate failures of equipment or of services (e.g., 
water, gas) to equipment, where necessary: 

Check visually all pieces of equipment to detect any scratches, dents or 
signs of damage:
a)
Do this systematically and regularly, 
b)
Evaluate and document each sign of damage (or temporal 
variation, respectively) according to a risk-based approach, 
irrespectively of whether or not product quality is directly impacted,
c)
Withdraw defective equipment identified as such from use, e.g., by 
labeling as defect until the defect has been rectified (based on the 
outcome of the evaluation),
d)
Perform all necessary requalification and recalibration activities 
successfully.

Prevent any hazard to the quality of the products during repair and 
maintenance operations.

Clean, sanitize and/or sterilize production equipment (incl. utility/process 
systems) according to detailed approved procedures:

Store those appropriately to maintain fitness for use (i.e., in a manner 
that will prevent re-contamination, microbiological contamination, the

components),

Ensure that the clean hold time of equipment is not exceeded before it 
is used. 

Avoid the use of glass equipment in the manufacturing of liquids, creams, 
and ointments [12] (i.e., high quality stainless steel). 

Apply the following requirements for Single Use Systems:

Apply GSOP 185 “Qualify GMP Material Supplier (API, excipient,

classified as critical disposable.

Inspect single use components for integrity where these are exposed 
to extreme conditions (e.g., freezing and thawing processes) during 
routine processing. Include intrinsic sterile connection devices (heat 
sealed and mechanically sealed) if existing [9].
GSOP 185
5.2.3
Closed Systems

Ensure the sterility of all product contact surfaces of closed systems used 
for aseptic processing [9].

Design and select only closed systems ensuring maintenance of sterility 
[9].

__data__.text:

Inspect single use components for integrity where these are exposed 
to extreme conditions (e.g., freezing and thawing processes) during 
routine processing. Include intrinsic sterile connection devices (heat 
sealed and mechanically sealed) if existing [9].
GSOP 185
5.2.3
Closed Systems

Ensure the sterility of all product contact surfaces of closed systems used 
for aseptic processing [9].

Design and select only closed systems ensuring maintenance of sterility 
[9].

Design the connection of sterile equipment (e.g., tubing/pipework) to the 
sterilized product pathway, after the final sterilizing grade filter, aseptically 
(e.g., by intrinsic sterile connection devices) [9].

Implement appropriate measures ensuring the integrity of components 
used in aseptic connections [9]. 

Consider appropriate system integrity tests when there is a risk of 
compromising product sterility [9]. 

Base the background environment in which closed systems are located on 
their design and the processes undertaken (see also GSOP 268 “Quality 
Requirements for Clean Rooms” [9].
Production Personnel
GSOP 268
5.2.4
Test Runs for Production Machines

Carry out test runs on/with equipment/machines which are normally used 
for production of marketed drug products, Medical Devices, or clinical 
samples, e.g.:

To test the usability of a particular machine (e.g., selection of an 
alternative equipment),

To evaluate new production material (e.g., packaging materials) or

During equipment qualification (typically OQ).

Discard test product(s).

Avoid mix-up and cross-contamination of batches intended for the market 
or clinical trials with products from test runs.

Document all essential steps, involved functions, responsible persons, 
necessary amount of materials and dates of testing (e.g. in a working plan, 
batch record, test report etc.). 

Mark the respective area where the test run takes place clearly (when the 
test runs are carried out). 

Ensure that the test product is clearly distinguishable from product for the 
market (e.g., visually, by appropriate labelling, by using specific material 
codes etc.).
Production Personnel


Package test products (incl. placebos) preferably in unprinted packaging 
materials:

Define special precautions which are strictly followed in case authentic 
packaging material is inevitable (e.g., when testing authentic 
packaging material itself).

Alter all test products and material remainders unusable before being 
disposed. 

Label waste containers for product to be disposed (both test and regular) 
clearly: 

Held those under quarantine, physically separated from product 
destined for the market or in clinical trials.

Follow the instructions of GOI 565 “Manage Scrapping”. 

Reconcile the quantities of any product and packaging material used or 
discarded.


Alter all test products and material remainders unusable before being 
disposed. 

Label waste containers for product to be disposed (both test and regular) 
clearly: 

Held those under quarantine, physically separated from product 
destined for the market or in clinical trials.

Follow the instructions of GOI 565 “Manage Scrapping”. 

Reconcile the quantities of any product and packaging material used or


Ensure that the requirements mentioned above are described in a test 
protocol for machine test runs which are carried out by third parties:

Provide the test protocol to the third party and

Get confirmation of the third party performing the test runs. 
Remarks:

Test runs are not considered validation runs (or PQ-runs, where 
applicable).

Test product(s) must not be placed on the market or used in clinical trials. 

Manufacturing activities and trials performed during process development 
are not subject of this section.
GOI 565
5.3
Material 
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference
5.3.1
Material Handling 

Handle and record (as necessary) materials and products, such as receipt, 
quarantine, sampling, storage, dispensing, processing, and distribution in 
accordance with authorized procedures or instructions.

For goods movements within the manufacturing site follow the 
instructions of GSOP 1590 “Manage Stock”.

General warehousing requirements for materials handling during 
storage are described in GSOP 267 “Warehousing Requirements”. 

Only released materials, which are within their expiration date or retest 
period, are used [5].

Dispense of drug products and materials is only being done by authorized 
personnel:

Correct materials are accurately weighed or measured into clean and 
properly labelled containers,

Check and record independently each dispensed material and its 
weight or volume, 

Keep together and conspicuously label materials dispensed for each 
batch [5].

Follow the requirements for material destruction and scrapping according 
to GOI 565 “Manage Scrapping”
Production Personnel, 
QC Personnel

INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &

5.3.2
Microbiological Provisions

Protect products and materials from microbiological contamination at every 
stage of processing.

Carry out processes such as compounding and formulation in a way to 
reduce the risk of microbial contamination, e.g., using closed containers for 
formulation and transfer [5].

Subject each batch of a component [8], Drug Product container or closure 
with potential for microbiological contamination that is objectionable in view 
of its intended use to microbiological tests (e.g., bioburden, endotoxin [8]) 
before use [14].
Remarks:

Requirements for validation of sterilization processes are described in 
GSOP 197 “Qualify / Validate Sterilization Process”.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

__data__.text:

Subject each batch of a component [8], Drug Product container or closure 
with potential for microbiological contamination that is objectionable in view 
of its intended use to microbiological tests (e.g., bioburden, endotoxin [8]) 
before use [14].
Remarks:

Requirements for validation of sterilization processes are described in 
GSOP 197 “Qualify / Validate Sterilization Process”.
Production Personnel 
GSOP 197
5.3.3
Bioburden

Monitor the bioburden on each batch for both aseptically filled and 
terminally sterilized products [9] at least from the bulk product [9] and prior 
to the final sterilization step [10][8]:

In case a pre-sterilizing filter is additionally installed, then sampling for 
bioburden testing may be performed prior to the pre-filtration, provided 
that no holding time is scheduled for the solution between the two 
filtration steps.

In case a redundant filtration set-up is used, take bioburden sample 
prior to the first filter [9].

Where overkill sterilization parameters are set for terminally sterilized 
products, bioburden is monitored only at suitable scheduled intervals 
according to a risk assessment. 

Design systems for taking samples not introducing contamination [9].

Perform the bioburden assay on each batch for parametric release 
systems and consider this test as an in-process test [9].
Production Personnel
5.3.4
Products Susceptible to Theft 

In principle every (new) substance or product is initially recognized not to 
be at special risk with respect to potential theft or misuse unless different 
data and/or information is available according to the provisions of GSOP 
142 “Perform Product Design and Development”.

Process PsT in such a way that unauthorized or casual access while 
walking by is reasonably avoided at all times by implementing physical 
barriers and restricted access.

Store PsT never with other products in the same room without a physical 
barrier protecting PsT unless the other products are also subject to special 
attendance/handling at a similar safeguard level like e.g., controlled 
drugs/controlled drug substances: 

Warehouses with no or restricted personnel entrance (highly 
automated usually high-bay warehouses) and already commissioned 
goods (different products on one pallet) are exempted from this 
requirement.

Document a change of responsibility (handover) in the chain of custody 
appropriately while processing PsT.
Production Personnel 
GSOP 142


Manufacture PsT by either with two persons present (four-eyes principle) 
or with one single person present subject to video surveillance: 

Video surveillance might be subject to local legislation and is therefore 
only implemented if all corresponding local legislation has been 
fulfilled.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls
Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls
Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

__data__.text:

Avoid unattended temporary availability while processing PsT unless PsT 
is subject to a physical barrier and/or the area is subject to a restricted 
access:

Store PsT in sealed containers to indicate an opening during the 
unattended period if PsT is unattended for a longer time period or 
transported outside of controlled areas, 

For this purpose, for instance tamper evident void bands, different kind 
of seals, special transport container and special closings/covers are 
available. 

Justify waste and rejects during processing, carefully evaluate and record.

Ensure maintenance of the marker “pilferable” in SAP for all PsT (if SAP is 
available):

Use an appropriate marker to indicate all PsT in the locally available IT 
system as an alternative (if SAP is not available).

Perform a detailed written risk assessment if an alternative method of 
control delivering a similar level to safeguard PsT is proposed (in case the 
defined safeguards shall not be fully implemented):

Document this alternative method and provide it upon request. 
Remark:

A list of APIs and products considered as PsT is provided in Supplement 
348 “List of Products susceptible to Theft (PsT)” and marked in SAP or in 
the corresponding local system as appropriate if SAP is not available.
Local responsible 
person for PsT
SUP 348
5.4
Processes and Methods
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference
5.4.1
Manufacturing

Perform production (incl. packaging) of APIs, Drug Products, the 
intermediates of both, investigational medicinal products (IMPs), Food 
Supplements and Medical Devices as well as in-process controls according 
to clearly defined authorized procedures and instructions. This is to ensure:

Compliance with documents submitted to authorities, and

That all quality specification requirements are met.

Ensure that the batch records or DHR are evaluated and signed by a 
responsible person for production before they are sent to the Quality Unit 
[19] for drug products. 

Carry out checks – where required – to ensure that pipelines, tubing, and 
other pieces of equipment used for transportation of products from one 
area to another are connected in a correct manner [5].

Do not blend Out-Of-Specification batches with other batches for the 
purpose of meeting specifications: 

In-process mixing of fractions from single batches (e.g., collecting 
several centrifuge loads from a single crystallization batch) or 
combining fractions from several batches for further processing is 
Production Personnel

to be blending [7].

Use recovered materials such as solvents, reagents, and catalysts used in 
the manufacturing process only in the same step or in an earlier step (if 
there is sufficient purification) of the same process from which it was 
collected [17].

The recovered materials meet appropriate specifications before reuse, 

Justify via a risk assessment the use of recovered/recycled solvents for 
the manufacturing of herbal substances.
Remarks:

The processing of materials in production generally refers to batch 
manufacturing.

__data__.text:

Use recovered materials such as solvents, reagents, and catalysts used in 
the manufacturing process only in the same step or in an earlier step (if 
there is sufficient purification) of the same process from which it was 
collected [17].

The recovered materials meet appropriate specifications before reuse, 

Justify via a risk assessment the use of recovered/recycled solvents for 
the manufacturing of herbal substances.
Remarks:

The processing of materials in production generally refers to batch 
manufacturing.

The combination of standard operating units (e.g., granulation, tableting, 
coating) in continuous manufacturing lines requires an in-depth 
understanding of each subunit and the overall system:

Quality attributes of intermediate product quality (e.g., granule quality, 
blend uniformity, etc.), settings of process parameters, application of 
online analytical test methods (PAT), and measurement of equipment 
performance are used to achieve a pre-defined state of control.

Batches of APIs, Drug Products and Medical Devices manufactured in the 
U.S. that are intended to be exported outside the U.S. need to be identified 
prior to the start of manufacturing, and clearly indicated as such unless 
they meet the U.S. specification.
5.4.2
Batch Management

To support the batch management processes, batch numbers need to 
follow a standard set of rules. These ensure the uniqueness of numbers 
across Bayer and provide the basis for being able to accept batch numbers 
from other sites.

Ensure each batch has a unique identifier.

Ensure the batch number is unique across all materials. If this is not 
possible in justified cases, then at least the combination of a batch number 
and a material number must be unique.

Consider, for production steps which are completely managed within one 
site, batch management processes can follow existing local procedures for 
allocating batch numbers, e.g., for changes of batch number for particular 
process steps, etc. as long as the general requirements for traceability as 
described in GSOP 588 "Manage Batch Traceability Request" are fulfilled.

Ensure, in specific cases, when it is not possible to implement all 
requirements defined in chapter 5.4.2.1, e.g. due to legacy computerized 
systems that do not allow the implementation with reasonable efforts, an 
alternative process from this chapter including an impact assessment for 
the affected supply chains must be documented. The overall traceability 
over the supply chain must still be ensured. For more guidance follow the 
general rules for generating Batch Numbers in chapter 5.4.2.1.
GSOP 588

5.4.2.1
Rules for generating Batch Numbers

Allow all English uppercase alphanumeric characters. This character set 
may be limited locally e.g., due to limitations in the printing equipment, as 
long as the requirements for incoming batches can still be fulfilled, see 
GSOP 1589 “Receive Goods”.

Ensure batch numbers are biunique and follow an established numbering 
system which is traced and documented.

__data__.text:
This character set 
may be limited locally e.g., due to limitations in the printing equipment, as 
long as the requirements for incoming batches can still be fulfilled, see 
GSOP 1589 “Receive Goods”.

Ensure batch numbers are biunique and follow an established numbering 
system which is traced and documented.

Ensure batch numbers contain not more than 10 digits

Ensure only Arabic Numbers and Roman Alphabets are used

Apply the following rules for all internal batch management processes 
managed in SAP:

Add a maximum of two suffixes with one alphanumeric character each 
for repackaging activities. An example is shown in the figure below.

Batch numbers consist of a 2-digit prefix defined once per 
organizational unit or legal entity and 5 alphanumeric characters. 
The prefixes are assigned on a Bayer Corporate level by the 
corresponding Master Data management team.

Print batch numbers on primary and printed secondary packaging. On each 
secondary packaging material only one batch number is printed. Specific 
rules for combination packs are given in GSOP 209 “Requirements for 
Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Materials”.

Ensure printed batch numbers and stock-keeping batch numbers 
maintained in the documentation and any used IT systems are identical for 
any material produced in-house.
Remarks:

Contract manufacturer or packager use batch numbers meeting the same 
rules as described above. The specific requirements to be applied are 
aligned in the respective QAA. It must be considered that the batch number 
mentioned above (AB01245XY) is the preferred option. In all cases the 
provided batch number needs to ensure a seamless takeover to Bayer 
internal systems.

If specific regulatory requirements or contractors demand the use of special 
batch numbering formats, then this is documented locally and 
communicated to the affected supply chain partners.

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference
5.4.3
Manufacturing Date

Consider the following provisions for Drug Products:

Define the date of manufacture of a batch as the date of the first 
combining, blending, or mixing the API (active ingredient) with other 
ingredients (either APIs or excipients).

Take the initial date of the filling operation as the date of manufacture 
for Drug Products consisting of a single active ingredient filled into 
containers.

Take the date of the first combining, blending, or mixing of at least two 
components as the date of manufacture for Drug Products free of API 
(placebos). 

Consider the following provisions for Biologics:

Define the date of initiation of the last valid potency test as the date of 
manufacture for products for which an official standard of potency is 
prescribed, or which are subject to official potency tests.

Define the date of removal from animals, the date of extraction, the 
date of solution, the date of cessation of growth, or the date of the final 
sterile filtration of a bulk solution as date of manufacture for products 
that are not subject to official potency tests.

Consider the following provisions for APIs:

Define the date of manufacture as the date that the last manufacturing 
step (except release testing) is completed.

__data__.text:

Define the date of removal from animals, the date of extraction, the 
date of solution, the date of cessation of growth, or the date of the final 
sterile filtration of a bulk solution as date of manufacture for products 
that are not subject to official potency tests.

Consider the following provisions for APIs:

Define the date of manufacture as the date that the last manufacturing 
step (except release testing) is completed.
a)
Consider physical processing steps such as micronization and 
sieving as well as the final filling as manufacturing steps.
b)
Based on the process and product characteristics, a different 
manufacturing step may be defined. 

Consider the following provisions for Medical Devices:

Define the date of manufacture based on the specific product.
Remarks:

For Food Supplements the requirements for manufacturing date are stated 
in GSOP 1180 “GMP/GDP Requirements for Food Supplements/Dietary 
Supplements”.

The following exemptions overrule the above described provisions: 
Definitions for the manufacturing date provided:

In the marketing authorization,

In the clinical trial authorization,

In registration documents,

By country-specific and/or local requirements.
Production Personnel
GSOP 1180
5.4.4
Expiry Date 

Consider the following provisions for Drug Products:

Calculate the expiry date from the manufacturing date plus shelf life 
(independently of the release date) unless otherwise defined by country-
specific local legislation.

Express it as MM/YYYY if not otherwise defined in the marketing 
authorization (The product expires at the end of the specified month).
Production Personnel

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference
5.4.5
Sterile/Aseptic Manufacturing

Pass all solutions to be used in sterile formulations (irrespectively of 
whether or not they are terminally sterilized later on [9]) through a micro-
organism-retaining filter [1] (if product composition / viscosity allows 
filtration), whenever possible sited immediately before filling [9].

Ensure, during a manufacturing process, that products or components 
intended to be sterilized are clearly distinguishable from those which have 
already been sterilized.

Label each basket, tray, or other carrier of products or components clearly 
with the name of the material, its batch number, and an indication of 
whether or not it has already been sterilized (e.g., autoclave tape) [10].

Provide careful attention, to the environment, personnel, critical surfaces, 
container/closure sterilization and transfer procedures, the maximum 
holding period of the product before filling and the sterilizing filter, to 
maintain the sterility of components and/or the product during aseptic 
processing [1].

Moist Heat Sterilization:

Ensure a weekly leak test on the sterilizer (moist heat sterilization) e.g., 
autoclave, is implemented and executed [9]:
a)
in case a vacuum phase is part of the cycle or
b)
the system is returned (post-sterilization) to a pressure lower than 
the environment surrounding the sterilizer.

Implement and execute a daily air removal test or install an air detector 
system when the sterilization process includes purging (e.g., porous 
autoclave loads, lyophilizer chambers) [9].

Ensure a system that has been sterilized by steam in place remains 
integral. Ensure the system is maintained under positive pressure or 
equip with a sterilizing vent filter prior to use in case this is 
operationally required [9].
Production Personnel
5.4.6
Sterile Filtration (Product Filtration)

Refer to GSOP 197 “Qualify/Validate Sterilization Process” regarding 
requirements related to implementation and validation of sterile filtration.

Sterilize membrane filters either off-line or in-line: 

Off-Line Sterilization:
a)
Verify steam penetration in case sterilization is executed off-line.
b)
Protect the sterilized filter suitably against contamination.

__data__.text:
Production Personnel
5.4.6
Sterile Filtration (Product Filtration)

Refer to GSOP 197 “Qualify/Validate Sterilization Process” regarding 
requirements related to implementation and validation of sterile filtration.

Sterilize membrane filters either off-line or in-line: 

Off-Line Sterilization:
a)
Verify steam penetration in case sterilization is executed off-line.
b)
Protect the sterilized filter suitably against contamination.
c)
Assemble the sterilized filter aseptically in the production line by 
means of a validated procedure.

In-Line Sterilization:
a)
Assure steam penetration throughout the filtration equipment.
b)
Control the pressure difference across the membrane preventing 
damage to the membrane itself.

Carry out final sterile filtration as close as possible to the filling point [1].

Justify by means of a risk evaluation, in case that the final sterile 
filtration is not performed in a close distance to the filling point.

State and minimize the maximum time between the start of bulk solution 
preparation and sterile filtration appropriately supported by data [16]:

Justify filtration times longer than 24 hours,


Repeat the sterile filtration immediately before filling, if a sterile filtered 
bulk solution is not filled into the final containers within 24 hours 
(unless justified by a risk assessment),

Perform an additional bioburden test before any further bioburden 
reduction step after the holding time is exceeded,

Justify the holding time adequately.

Implement a double-filter layer (or second filtration through a further 
sterilized microorganism-retaining filter) immediately prior to filling in case 
of further aseptic processing due to the potential risks of the filtration 
method compared with other sterilization processes.

Discard liquid sterilizing grade filters after processing of a single batch and 
ensure the same filter is not used continuously for more than one working 
day unless such use has been validated [1][21][9].

Implement controls ensuring filters contaminated with fluid or cleaning 
agent residues, or considered defective in any other way, are removed 
from use [9].
5.4.6.1
Filter Integrity Test including PUPSIT

General aspects for Filter Integrity Test:

Validate the integrity test process and confirm that the test results 
correlate with the microbial retention capability of the filter established 
during validation.

Implement integrity test methods as bubble point, diffusive flow, water 
intrusion or pressure hold test.

Verify the integrity of the sterilized filter before use.

Confirm the integrity of the sterilized filter immediately after use by an 
appropriate method (e.g., bubble point test, diffusive flow or pressure 
hold test).

Consider the filtration system as a single sterilizing unit in case the 
sterilizing filtration process is validated as a system consisting of 
multiple filters to achieve the sterility for a given fluid [9]. 
a)
Ensure all filters within the system are integrity tested after use. 
b)
All filters must pass the integrity test.

__data__.text:

Verify the integrity of the sterilized filter before use.

Confirm the integrity of the sterilized filter immediately after use by an 
appropriate method (e.g., bubble point test, diffusive flow or pressure 
hold test).

Consider the filtration system as a single sterilizing unit in case the 
sterilizing filtration process is validated as a system consisting of 
multiple filters to achieve the sterility for a given fluid [9]. 
a)
Ensure all filters within the system are integrity tested after use. 
b)
All filters must pass the integrity test.

Perform a post-use integrity test of the primary sterilizing grade filter in 
a redundant filtration system (where a second redundant sterilizing 
grade filter is present as a backup, but the sterilizing process is 
validated as only requiring one filter) [9]:
a)
Post-use integrity test of the redundant (backup) filter is not 
necessary in case the primary filter has been demonstrated to be 
integral.
b)
Perform the post-use integrity test on the secondary (redundant) 
filter in the event of a failure of the post-use integrity test on the 
primary filter.
c)
Determine the reason in case of a test failure for the primary filter 
initiating an investigation and risk assessment. Apply GSOP 114 
“Manage Deviation (Nonconformity)”.

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference

Pre-Use Post Sterilization test (PUPSIT) applied only for aseptic 
processing (final sterile filtration):

Verify the integrity of the sterilized filter by integrity testing post 
sterilization (PUPSIT), conforming that there was no damage and loss 
of integrity caused by the preparation.

Confirm the integrity of the sterilized filter immediately after use by an 
appropriate non-destructive method (e.g., bubble point test, diffusive 
flow or pressure hold test) prior to removal of the filter from its housing.

In case of process or product constrains, PUPSIT may not be possible, 
and confirmation of filter integrity is only performed after use:
a)
Perform a risk assessment ensuring compliance is achieved by 
implementing appropriate controls to mitigate any risk of a non-
integral filtration.
b)
Consider at the minimum the following points in the risk 
assessment:
(i)
Demonstrate knowledge and control of the filter 
sterilization process ensuring the potential for damage to 
the filter is minimized.
(ii) Demonstrate control of the filter supply chain including 
contract sterilization facilities, filter transport, filter 
packaging (prevent filter damage during transport and 
storage).
(iii) Assess the risk of impacting filter integrity values including 
altering integrity-test values (e.g., product type, particle 
burden). This includes the risk to prevent the detection of a 
non-integral filter.
(iv) Include pre-filtration and processing steps prior final 
sterilization able to remove e.g., particle burden.
5.4.7
Gas and Air Vent Filters

Verify the integrity of critical gas and air vent filters (e.g., directly linked to 
the sterility of the product) upon installation [8] and confirm after use, with 
the filter remaining in the filter assembly or housing [9].

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

__data__.text:
This includes the risk to prevent the detection of a 
non-integral filter.
(iv) Include pre-filtration and processing steps prior final 
sterilization able to remove e.g., particle burden.
5.4.7
Gas and Air Vent Filters

Verify the integrity of critical gas and air vent filters (e.g., directly linked to 
the sterility of the product) upon installation [8] and confirm after use, with 
the filter remaining in the filter assembly or housing [9].

Apply the following approach for the integrity verification of non-critical air 
or gas vent filters (e.g., police filters):

Aseptic Process:
Verify the integrity at appropriate intervals [9], at least prior to use and 
every time when replaced (e.g., due to maintenance) to assure that it 
was not compromised and has been working properly since installation:
a)
Gas filters which are in place for extended periods, integrity testing 
is carried out at installation and prior to replacement.
b)
Specify and monitor the maximum duration of use based on risk 
(e.g., consider the maximum number of uses, heat treatment/ 
sterilization cycles permitted)

Sterile Process, Low Bioburden/Bioburden Controlled Process:
Verify the integrity at appropriate intervals following a risk-based 
approach.

Avoid for gas filtration unintended moistening or wetting of the filter or filter 
equipment [9].

Confirm the integrity of other filters, based on a risk assessment, at 
appropriate intervals [9].
Production Personnel

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference

Consider an increased monitoring of filter integrity in processes that involve 
harsh conditions, e.g., the circulation of high-temperature air [1].
5.4.8
Lyophilization

Apply the following requirements for lyophilization [9]:

Sterilize the lyophilizer regularly, based on system design. 

Perform re-sterilization following maintenance or cleaning activities. 

Protect sterilized lyophilizers and associated equipment from 
contamination after sterilization. 

Design lyophilizers and associated product transfer and 
loading/unloading areas to minimize operator intervention as far as 
possible.

Base the lyophilizer sterilization frequency on the design and 
contamination risk during use.
a)
Sterilize lyophilizers, that are manually loaded or unloaded with no 
barrier technology separation, before each load.

Check lyophilization trays regularly to ensure they are not misshapen 
or damaged.

Maintain the integrity of the lyophilizer following sterilization and during 
lyophilization.

Sterilize the filter used to maintain lyophilizer integrity before use of the 
system.

Document the frequency of vacuum/leak integrity testing of the 
chamber and check the maximum permitted leakage of air into the 
lyophilizer at the start of every cycle.
Production Personnel
5.4.9
Metal Check of Tablets, Powders and Suspensions

Apply a metal check routinely in order to eliminate potential contamination 
with metal particles either caused by the manufacturing process itself or 
originating from contaminated excipients, when producing drug products as 
tablets, powders, and suspensions for oral intake.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

__data__.text:

Document the frequency of vacuum/leak integrity testing of the 
chamber and check the maximum permitted leakage of air into the 
lyophilizer at the start of every cycle.
Production Personnel
5.4.9
Metal Check of Tablets, Powders and Suspensions

Apply a metal check routinely in order to eliminate potential contamination 
with metal particles either caused by the manufacturing process itself or 
originating from contaminated excipients, when producing drug products as 
tablets, powders, and suspensions for oral intake.

Apply metal check technology routinely for each tablet machine, except 
when other systems are used or processes are in place which are capable 
of defecting and eliminating contaminated product, e.g., at a later stage of 
the process (e.g., during packaging). 

Qualify metal check devices considering an appropriate limit of detection 
(e.g., 0.5 mm stainless steel particles, 0.5 mm ferrous particles, etc.):

Those limits are justified by corresponding health hazard assessments 
for potential process-inherent metal contamination below the 
established detection limit of the metal check device. 

Perform routine performance checks of metal check devices according to 
written procedures depending on the experiences gained during 
qualification as well as on routine use:

Document the results of the performance checks accordingly. 

Establish action levels (considering the results from the metal check 
qualification) for the respective products/processes including procedures 
on how to use the device and how to proceed in case of action level 
excursions: 

Accomplish corresponding failure investigations as necessary 
according to the applicable procedures.
Production Personnel

INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &


Consider establishing alert levels in case this is required from a risk-
based perspective.

Follow the applicable failure and/or deviation investigation procedures for 
certain incidents resulting in a potential metal contamination of tablets (e.g., 
accidental friction or shaving of metallic objects) irrespectively of the 
routine metal check procedure. 

Implement appropriate technical measures for suspensions to avoid any 
potential metal contamination:

Accomplish failure/deviation investigations according to the applicable 
procedures, where appropriate. 
5.4.10
Calculation of Yield

Consider the following provisions for API and Drug Product:

Determine actual yields and percentages of theoretical yield at the 
conclusions of each appropriate phase of manufacturing (including 
packaging), processing, or holding of the Drug Product or API.

Ensure that there are no discrepancies outside the acceptable limits. 

Record and investigate any deviation from the expected yield (limit or 
range, respectively, as claimed in the Master Batch Record).

Determine the impact or potential impact on the resulting quality of the 
affected batches. 

Consider the following provisions for Medical Devices:

Carry out checks on yields, and reconciliation of quantities (if 
necessary).

Ensure that there are no discrepancies outside the acceptable limits.
Production Personnel
5.4.11
Time Limits and Holding Times

Establish time limits for the completion of each phase of production (when 
appropriate) to assure the quality of bulk productions, APIs, including the 
intermediates of both, and medical device components.

__data__.text:

Consider the following provisions for Medical Devices:

Carry out checks on yields, and reconciliation of quantities (if 
necessary).

Ensure that there are no discrepancies outside the acceptable limits.
Production Personnel
5.4.11
Time Limits and Holding Times

Establish time limits for the completion of each phase of production (when 
appropriate) to assure the quality of bulk productions, APIs, including the 
intermediates of both, and medical device components. 

Confirm the maximum holding times for those materials during product 
validation, e.g., by using holding time as a process parameter which is 
investigated or monitored during the validation activity. 

Carry out chemical, physical, and microbiological testing (bioburden and 
endotoxin load as required [8]) directly after manufacture and at the end of 
the holding time considered to be practicable for subsequent production: 

It may also be beneficial to test after twice the length of the intended 
holding time.

Document validated holding times accordingly (e.g., by defining a “retest-
date”). 

Pay special attention to intermediates/bulk that are prone to microbial 
contamination, and to bulk that tends to be critical with regard to its further 
processing. This applies to e.g. [8]: 

The maximum period of storage and the storage conditions of liquids, 
creams, and ointments prior to filling [11],

The maximum time between the start of the preparation of a solution 
and its sterilization or filtration through a bacteria-retaining filter [1],

The maximum holding time of an aseptically processed solution before 
filling into its final container [14],
Production Personnel

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference

The maximum period of storage and the storage conditions of blends 
and granules prior to tableting [12] as well as of bulk prior to primary 
packaging. 

Define measures to be taken when holding times have been exceeded 
(e.g., re-filtration, re-blending, additional testing or rejection [12]).

Handle deviations from established time limits according to GSOP 114 
“Manage Deviation (Nonconformity)”. 

Define special requirements for storage of APIs, bulk storage of Drug 
Products and Medical Device components (e.g., to avoid electrostatic 
discharge), including the container, labelling and particular storage 
conditions, where necessary. 
Remarks:

Further details are provided in GSOP 136 “Requirements for Stability 
Study”.

The maximum time period between washing/drying/sterilization and use 
(“clean hold time”) is as short as possible and subject to validation; please 
refer to GSOP 242 “Requirements for Cleaning of Equipment in the 
Manufacture of APIs and Drug Products” for further details.
GSOP 114
GSOP 136
GSOP 242
5.4.12
In-Process Controls for API, Drug Product and Food Supplements 
(including combination products)

Perform IPCs either online or offline.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls
Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls
Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

__data__.text:

The maximum time period between washing/drying/sterilization and use 
(“clean hold time”) is as short as possible and subject to validation; please 
refer to GSOP 242 “Requirements for Cleaning of Equipment in the 
Manufacture of APIs and Drug Products” for further details.
GSOP 114
GSOP 136
GSOP 242
5.4.12
In-Process Controls for API, Drug Product and Food Supplements 
(including combination products)

Perform IPCs either online or offline.

Perform inspection and testing either by monitoring the process or by 
sample analysis at defined locations and times e.g., at beginning or 
completion of significant phases, during defined intervals or after storage of 
long periods.

Perform tests that are crucial for quality control but that cannot be carried 
out on the finished product at an appropriate stage of production: 

IPCs may be carried out within the production area, if they do not have 
any negative effect on the quality of the designated product or of other 
products.

Perform all sampling activities and IPCs, including those made in the 
production area by production personnel or QC personnel, after 
appropriate training according to written instructions and methods 
approved by the accountable Quality Assurance Unit. 

Document IPC results as described in GSOP 257 “Manage GMP/GDP 
Data Lifecycle”: 

Manage IPC results as raw data,

Ensure that IPC results are also available in the eBR.

Establish IPC-specifications on a scientifically sound basis. 

Describe in written and approved procedures actions to be taken when 
IPC-specification limits are exceeded depending on the type of IPC:

Repeat the respective process within the predefined ranges until the 
specified limit is achieved (e.g., pH adjustment of a solution), if the 
purpose of an IPC is to adjust a particular process step.

Handle otherwise exceeded IPC-specification limits, if relevant for 
product quality assessment, according to GSOP 113 “Manage Out of 
Specification Result” and 114 “Manage Deviation (Nonconformity)”. 

Identify, calibrate, and maintain properly all used IPC-instruments and test 
equipment.
QC Personnel, 
Production Personnel
GSOP 257
GSOP 113
GSOP 114

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference

Validate processes and methods used for IPC testing, where required 
according to GSOP 123 “Validate Analytical Procedure”.

Establish control points according to HACCP for Food Supplements.
Remarks: 

For PAT:

See Annex 1 for more details about different interfacing modes.

The control of the environment or the microbiological control of surfaces 
may also be regarded as a part of IPC for sterile or aseptic manufacturing.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls
Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls
Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

__data__.text:

Establish control points according to HACCP for Food Supplements.
Remarks: 

For PAT:

See Annex 1 for more details about different interfacing modes.

The control of the environment or the microbiological control of surfaces 
may also be regarded as a part of IPC for sterile or aseptic manufacturing. 

In-process control procedures may include, but are not limited to, the 
following:

Weight variation or uniformity of mass of tablet or capsules;

Disintegration time of solid dosage forms;

Friability of uncoated tablets;

Dissolution time, profile or rate of solid dosage forms;

Particle size distribution of crystalline solids and mixtures;

Clarity, completeness, or pH of solutions;

Moisture content of crystalline solids, mixtures, granules or 
intermediates;

Bioburden testing of solutions prior to sterile filtration. 
GSOP 123
5.4.13
In-Process Control for Medical Devices (including combination 
products)

Develop, conduct, control and monitor production processes to ensure that 
a Medical Device conforms to its specification. 

Establish process control procedures, where deviations (nonconformities) 
from specifications may occur, that describe any process controls 
necessary to ensure conformance to specifications. 

Include the following details (if process controls are needed):

Documented instructions, standard operating procedures (SOPs), and 
methods that define and control the manner of production, labelling and 
packaging;

Monitoring and control process parameters, and component and device 
characteristics during production;

Compliance with specified reference standards;

The approval of processes and process equipment; and

Criteria for workmanship which are expressed in documented 
standards or by means of identified and approved representative 
samples.
Production Personnel

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
5.5
Manufacturing Documentation
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference
5.5.1
Manufacturing Description

Include at least the following aspects to the manufacturing description:

Description of the pharmaceutical dosage form and strength of the 
product including weight or measure of each active ingredient per 
dosage unit (does neither apply to API syntheses nor to packaging 
processes),

Batch size, if applicable,

Brief description of the production process, and critical equipment 
operating conditions (e.g., temperature, pressure, pH, time),

Quantities or ranges of materials needed (does not apply to packaging 
processes),

General description of equipment used in production (e.g., dryer, 
ointment mixer, homogenizer),

IPC values/process monitoring and critical process parameters 
including the acceptance criteria,

Yield ranges,

Illustration of the production process in a flow chart.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls

__data__.text:
Production Personnel
5.5.2
Master Batch Record

Follow the provisions of GSOP 257 “Manage GMP/GDP Data Lifecycle” for 
the GMP-compliant documentation of manufacturing processes of Drug 
Products, APIs, Food Supplements and Medical Devices as well as 
general documentation and archiving requirements:

Ensure that Master Batch Records/Device Master Record are in 
compliance with the GMP requirements of the local Regulatory 
Authority, Technical Documentation/Design Dossier and with the 
specifications in the marketing authorization. 

Approve Master/DMR and executed Batch Records/DHR and 
associated records.

Prepare Master Batch Records (including all supporting documentation) 
following the process for creation and managing of forms as described in 
GSOP 257 “Manage GMP/GDP Data Lifecycle” ensuring the 
manufacturing process is performed according to defined procedures and 
instructions. There procedures are denoted as Master Batch Records (also 
designated as Manufacturing Formula/Processing Instructions, Packaging 
Instructions, Master Production/Packaging Record or Master 
Production/Packaging Instructions, etc.).

Include to the Master Batch Record:

A version number,

The name of the API, Drug Product, IMP, incl. the respective 
intermediates, to be manufactured and a corresponding material 
number or any other reference code relating to the specification,

The batch size to which the processing instructions apply (if 
applicable),

A description of the pharmaceutical dosage form and strength of the 
product (where appropriate),

The name and weight or measure of each active ingredient per dosage 
unit or per unit of weight or measure of the drug product, and a 
statement of the total weight or measure of any dosage unit, where 
appropriate. In addition, Master Batch Records for packaging 
Production Personnel;

weight, or volume of the product in the final container,

A statement of the processing location, the major equipment to be 
used and the assignment of the different manufacturing steps to the


Instructions to check that (1) the equipment and work station are clear




Materials or components to be used, and the amount, weight, or

also a statement concerning any calculated excess of component:
a)
If the amount of an API to be used for production is re-calculated 
based on its “assay for use” the respective procedure will have to 
be described.

__data__.text:
It may also be necessary to mention any substance 
that may disappear in the course of processing (e.g., water, 
solvents),
b)
For packaging processes: A complete and descriptive list of all 
packaging materials required for a standard batch size, including 
quantities, sizes and types, with the code or reference number 
relating to the specifications of each packaging material, 
additionally, where appropriate, an example or reproduction of the 
relevant printed packaging materials and labels, and specimens 
(or reference to instructions and approvals) indicating where and 
how (data format) to apply the batch number and the expiry date 
to the batch. 

Detailed stepwise description or reference to the description of the 
manufacturing process or packaging operation, including checks on 
materials, processing instructions, pre-treatments, sequence for 
adding materials, critical process parameters, process-determined 
limits or ranges and any significant subsidiary operations,

Instructions, or reference to instructions (e.g., approved sampling 
plans and test procedures) for sampling and performing any in-process 
controls and/or process analytical technology (PAT), as well as the 
specification limits (unless elsewhere defined) and – where 
appropriate – the type of equipment to be used,

A statement of theoretical weight or measure at appropriate phases of 
processing, and a statement of the theoretical (in API production: 
expected) final yield, where applicable of relevant intermediate yields 
and the respective acceptable limits,

Where applicable, the maximum holding times of bulk products, APIs 
and the intermediates of both (or a reference to separate 
documentation),

Where appropriate, the requirements for bulk storage of the products, 
including the container, labelling and any special storage conditions (or 
a reference to separate documentation), 

Any other special precautions to be observed.

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference

Ensure the Master Batch Record is approved by the organizational unit 
and QA.

Ensure all changes to the Master Batch Record are managed according to 
the provisions of GSOP 503 “Manage Change”.
GSOP 503
5.5.3
Batch Record

Describe and specify the manufacturing process for an individual batch in 
a Batch Record (also designated as Batch Processing/Packaging Record 
or Batch Production/Packaging Record etc.):

Manage batch records as Working Copies in accordance with GSOP 
257 “Manage GMP/GDP Data Lifecycle”,

Ensure the Batch Record is based on the corresponding approved 
Master Batch Record,

The transcription method of preparing such Batch Records follows 
written procedures approved by QA to avoid transcription errors and to 
ensure integrity, accuracy, and traceability,

Each page of the paper-based Batch Record contains the name of the 
product to be manufactured and the batch number (usually pre-
printed/-stamped),

In addition to the contents of the Master Batch Record, the Batch 
Record contains the indication of batch size/quantity of units to be 
actually produced (if applicable) as well as the determination of the 
equipment to be used,

Batch Packaging Records also stipulate the batch number and 
quantity of bulk product to be packaged.
Production Personnel
GSOP 257
5.5.4
Executed Batch Record

Document the actual manufacturing process for a specific batch in an 
Executed Batch Record.

__data__.text:
Production Personnel
GSOP 257
5.5.4
Executed Batch Record

Document the actual manufacturing process for a specific batch in an 
Executed Batch Record.

Manage data and entries in the Executed Batch Record as raw data.

Record, during processing, the following information at the date and time 
each relevant action is taken:

The batch numbers and amount of all materials used (including the 
batch numbers and amount of any recovered or reprocessed material 
added) For packaging processes:
a)
The batch numbers, quantities and reference number or 
identification code of all printed packaging materials and bulk 
products used, destroyed, or returned to stock,
b)
The quantities of obtained product, in order to provide for an 
adequate reconciliation.

The inspection of facilities, equipment, work station, packaging and 
labelling area(s) before and, where applicable after use (demonstration 
of “line clearance”),

The major equipment (including rooms, utility use points, weighing 
equipment and containers if appropriate) and - for packaging 
processes – the packaging lines actually used, identified by a 
distinctive identification number or code,

Weights and measures of components used in the course of 
processing, if a component/intermediate is removed from one 
container to another, also the weight and measure in the new 
container,

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference

Details of any (other) relevant processing or packaging operations and 
activities carried out including all relevant and critical process 
parameters set and measured/recorded,

The signature of the operator(s) who performed the activities and set 
the production parameters and the signature of the person who 
checked each significant step in operations,

Dates and times of commencement, of significant process 
interruptions, of significant intermediate stages and of completion of 
production and packaging operations (for API production: recording of 
times only required for critical process steps),

Name and signature of the person having been responsible 
(“supervising”) for each stage of production/packaging operation,

Any sampling activities performed,

IPC testing records including the methods applied, the results 
obtained, and the signature of the person(s) carrying out sampling, 
testing and double checking, unless elsewhere documented,

The quantities of obtained product and/or the amount of actual product 
yield and a statement of the percentage of theoretical (in API 
production: expected) yield obtained in order to provide for an 
adequate reconciliation; this applies to the final and – if appropriate – 
to intermediate stages of manufacture. For packaging processes:
a)
Samples of printed packaging materials (foil, leaflet, folding carton) 
and
b)
Labels used, including specimens of the batch coding, expiry date 
and any additional overprinting.

Include results of critical process parameters.

__data__.text:
For packaging processes:
a)
Samples of printed packaging materials (foil, leaflet, folding carton) 
and
b)
Labels used, including specimens of the batch coding, expiry date 
and any additional overprinting.

Include results of critical process parameters. 
For sterile/aseptic manufacturing include (but not limited to) the 
minimum time taken to filter a known volume of bulk solution and 
pressure difference across the filter (documented significant 
differences),

Include confirmation that results of environmental monitoring conform 
with defined limits (i.e., for temperature, relative humidity and other 
specific characteristics),

Records of checks for conformity with the instructions (Batch Record), 
and notes on any unusual events or deviation, and/or reference to 
separate deviation documentation, if applicable. 

Include for sterile/aseptic manufacturing the following additional 
information [9]:

Confirmation of conformity for each sterilization run,

Attach/Include integrity test results of product, gas and vent filter used 
for aseptic manufacturing (e.g., product vessel vent filter, gas filter at 
point of use),

Attach/Include integrity test results of the filter maintaining integrity of 
the lyophilizer,

Each line stoppage or intervention with the associated time, duration of 
the event, and operators involved. 

Handle any deviation from the Batch Record (e.g., percentage of 
theoretical yield exceeding the ranges established in the MBR, 
unexplained event or discrepancy, failure of a batch or any of its 
components to meet any of its specifications) according to GSOP 114 
“Manage Deviation (Nonconformity)”.

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
Description
Assigned Roles &
Document Reference

Review the Executed Batch Record after completion of the manufacturing 
process operations with respect to:
a)
Completeness (e.g., inclusion of associated documents where 
required),
b)
Form and content, 
c)
Dated and signed for approval by the person responsible for the 
processing operations and the responsible QA Unit.

Exemptions (e.g., API intermediates for further processing) have to 
comply with the requirements defined in GSOP 471 “Manage Release 
and Retesting”.

Use a review checklist to capture all documents that actually are, or 
deliberately are not, associated with a particular eBR:

This checklist helps also to identify additional authorized manual 
Forms generated during production, as well as to document 
completeness of the eBR itself.
Remarks:

Executed Batch Records also require QA unit review and approval. 

Where a validated process is continuously monitored and controlled, 
automatically generated reports may be limited to compliance summaries 
and deviations-/OOS-data reports.

If an MES is used, loose forms that are scanned and attached to an eBR 
should be avoided whenever possible by including the respective activities 
(e.g., documentation of functional test, cleaning activities, IPC results) into 
the Master Batch Record.

GSOP 471
5.5.5
Device Master Record (DMR)

Perform production of Medical Devices according to clearly defined and 
approved written procedures and instructions designed to ensure that the 
Medical Device has the required quality by following the provisions of 
GSOP 2041 “Perform Design Control for Medical Devices and 
Combination Products”.
Production Personnel
GSOP 2041
5.5.6
Device History Record (DHR)

Establish Device History Records for each batch or unit of a Medical 
Device to demonstrate that the device is manufactured in accordance with 
the corresponding Device Master Record and the requirements laid down 
therein. 

Include, or refer to the location of, at least the following information:

The dates of manufacture,

The quantity manufactured and the quantity released for distribution,

The acceptance records which demonstrate the device is 
manufactured in accordance with the DMR, including:
a)
The acceptance activities performed,
b)
The dates the acceptance activities were performed, 
c)
The results of the acceptance activities and the signature of the 
person(s) who conducted the acceptance activities,
d)
Where appropriate, also the equipment used for the acceptance 
activities.

The primary identification label and labelling used for each production 
unit,
Production Personnel

__data__.text:
13.0 | 131
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-03-15
Page: 36 of 38 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-23 17:30 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

Requirements for Production and In-Process Controls 
INTERNAL
9
Annex 1 Interfacing modes (PAT) 
The interfacing of analytical techniques with the manufacturing process is central to the application of 
PAT. Results generated by analytical equipment interfaced with a process can be used for monitoring 
purposes and to ensure that the process is stable, for example via automated feedback or feedforward 
control loops. The terms off-line, at-line, on-line and in-line describe interfacing modes. Measurements 
using on-line and in-line systems generally support rapid and automated process adjustments since 
they move the analytical techniques into the process stream. In contrast, off-line and at-line 
measurements involve transferring the samples away from the process stream to the analytical 
equipment. 
Off-line measurements:
These correspond to conventional analytical testing in which a sample is removed from the 
manufacturing process environment and tested in laboratory, typically located away from the 
production environment. This transfer of samples away from the process stream can result in a 
significant time lag and generally does not permit immediate process adjustments. 
At-line measurements:
With at-line measurements the sample is also removed (manually or automatically) from the process 
stream for testing, but the testing equipment is usually located within the production environment, i.e. 
in close physical proximity to the process stream, and testing can therefore take place with minimal 
delay. This results in a shorter time frame for obtaining analytical data, and also differentiates at-line 
measurements form off-line measurements. It is therefore possible to make process adjustments 
based on the results of at-line measurements. 
On-line measurements: 
On-line measurements typically sensor-based measurements made under real-time conditions by 
diverting a portion of the material form the process stream directly into a measuring device and the 
results are available after a minimal time delay. Depending on the nature of the test, e.g. whether or 
not it is detrimental to the product, the diverted portion may be returned to the process stream, 
otherwise it is discarded. 
In-line measurements:
In-line measurements are taken within the process stream by placing measuring devices, typically 
sensors, directly in the contact with or into the process stream. No portions of the material are therefore 
removed from the process stream. In-line measurements have to be non-detrimental to the product.

Manage GxP computerized system life cycle
RESTRICTED
1
Scope and Framework
1.1
Scope
This GSOP defines the general process of how to “Manage GxP computerized system life cycle”. 
It applies cross-functionally to all systems including relevant IT infrastructure implemented, operated, 
maintained or retired/decommissioned within the GxP regulated areas independent of whether they 
technically reside at Bayer, Bayer’s service providers or at a cloud provider (IaaS, PaaS or SaaS).
Not in scope of this procedure is 
-
software as an integral part of a medical device or classified itself as a medical device 
-
computerized systems used within the PH GLP and GCIS areas.
1.2
Framework
The process / documentation architecture is defined as below:
Manage IT Infrastructure Service Life 
Cycle (GSOP 2028)
Core Process of how to qualify IT Infrastructure Services 
and maintain their qualified state until retirement.
Set up and implement a 
computerized system in the GxP 
environment (GSOP 2003)
Core Process of to how set up and implement a new or 
updated computerized system in the GxP environment 
from initial planning to release into the productive 
environment.
Manage GxP computerized 
system in projects using agile 
methodologies (GOI 1699)
Variant of the process “Set up and implement a 
computerized system in the GxP environment” describing 
how validation deliverables are set up when projects work 
with agile methodologies.
Manage computerized system life 
cycle for GxP robotic process 
automation (GOI 1737)
Variant of the process “Set up and implement a 
computerized system in the GxP environment” describing 
how responsibilities are assigned for robotic process 
automation and which deliverables are necessary for the 
validation of such bots.
Manage computerized system life 
cycle for systems that use 
Artificial Intelligence / Machine 
Learning (GOI 1861)
Variant of the process “Set up and implement a 
computerized system in the GxP environment” describing 
how validation deliverables are set up when systems are

implemented 
that 
include 
artificial 
intelligence 
components.
Operate and maintain a GxP 
relevant computerized system 
(GSOP 2004)
Core Process of how to operate and maintain a system 
through its life cycle including changes, system issue

SAP Authorization Management

management is performed for systems that are using 
Bayer IdentityNow.
Data Integrity – Retrospective 
Review of Data (GOI 1405)
Variant of the process “Operate and maintain a GxP 
relevant computerized system” defining requirements of 
the retrospective review of data in computerized systems 
in order to identify potential data integrity breaches.
Retire and decommission a GxP-
relevant computerized system 
(GSOP 2005)
Core 
Process 
of 
how 
to 
manage 
retiring 
and 
decommissioning a GxP computerized system ensuring 
that relevant data is not lost.
Migrate a GxP-relevant 
computerized system 
(GSOP 2006)
Core Process of how to manage migration of data from one 
system to the next while ensuring data integrity.
2
Abbreviations
SaaS
Software as a Service
IaaS
Infrastructure as a Service
PaaS
Platform as a Service
QA
Quality Assurance
QAA
Quality Assurance Agreement
SLA
Service Level Agreement
IQMS-103486 Ver. 3.0 | 2002
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-12-15
Page: 5 of 19 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-21 16:32 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

Manage GxP computerized system life cycle
RESTRICTED
3
Definitions
For generic definitions see GMP/GDP QMS Supplement 200 “CMS Glossary”.
Specific definitions in the context of this SOP are provided below.
Computer System
A system containing one or more computers (hardware, firmware) and 
associated software.
Computerized 
System
A computerized system consists of the hardware, software, and network 
components, together with the controlled functions and associated 
documentation.
Figure 1: PIC/S Guidance - Good Practices for Computerized Systems in Regulated “GxP” 
Environments
Data Integrity
The extent to which all data are accurate, legible, contemporaneous, 
orginal and attributable (ALCOA) throughout the data lifecycle.
Local system 
Systems used only within one site or function and administered locally. 
Note: the same technology/software may be installed in different 
sites/areas, but these would still be local systems if maintained under 
control of each site/organization using it.
Global system
Systems used by more than one division and/or more than one function 
and/or is judged by the division or function to be business critical.
GxP
Is a general abbreviation for the “good practice” quality guidelines and 
regulations, the “GxP” stands for GLP, GMP/GDP, GCP, GVP 
Compliance requirements for all good practice disciplines in the 
regulated pharmaceutical sector supply chain from discovery to post 
marketing (PIC/S Guidance definition).
Predicate Rule
Synonym for the applicable set of GxP regulations, for example ICH-
Guidelines (GCP) or FDA CFR Code of Federal Regulations on Current 
Good Manufacturing Practices cGMP. This also includes country or 
region-specific regulations.

__data__.text:
Predicate Rule
Synonym for the applicable set of GxP regulations, for example ICH-
Guidelines (GCP) or FDA CFR Code of Federal Regulations on Current 
Good Manufacturing Practices cGMP. This also includes country or 
region-specific regulations.
