RESTRICTED
Electronic Record
Any combination of text, graphics, data, audio, pictorial, or other  
information representation in digital form that is created, modified, 
maintained, archived, retrieved, or distributed by a computer system.
In the sense of these SOPs, also referred to as GxP-regulated 
electronic record which is composed out of data that are required to be 
maintained under predicate rule requirements and that are maintained in 
electronic format in place of paper format.
Electronic Signature
A computer data compilation of any symbol or series of symbols 
executed, adopted, or authorized by an individual to be the legally 
binding equivalent of the individual’s handwritten signature. 
Consideration is given to the meaning of a signature. (according to 21 
CFR Part 11)
Electronic signatures that are intended to be the equivalent of 
handwritten signatures, initials, and other general signings required by 
predicate rules.
Category 1: 
Infrastructure 
Systems
There are two types of software in this category:

Established or commercially available software that can consist 
of several layers. Some of the examples are listed below. 
Applications are developed to run under the control of this 
software. This includes operating systems, database engines, 
programming languages, middleware, ladder logic interpreters, 
statistical programming tools, and standard business packages 
such as Microsoft Office (but not the GxP applications that are 
developed based on these programs). 

Infrastructure software tools include such tools as network 
monitoring software, batch job scheduling tools, security software 
or anti-virus tools.
Category 3: 
Non-configured 
Software
Non-configured software includes off-the-shelf products used for 
business purposes. It includes systems that cannot be configured 
to conform to business processes (although configuration of run-
time parameters is possible) and systems that are configurable but 
for which only the default configuration is used. 
Category 4: 
Configurable 
Software Packages
Configurable software products provide standard interfaces and 
functions that enable configuration of user specific business 
processes. This involves configuring predefined software modules. 
Category 4 includes commercially available systems only.
Category 5: 
Custom 
(Bespoke) 
Software
These systems or sub-systems are developed to meet the specific 
needs of the regulated company. 
Custom development may consist of a complete system or 
extension to an existing system. This category also includes 
configurable custom (bespoke) software as well as internally 
developed systems.
IT 
Infrastructure 
Service
An IT Service within IT Infrastructure environment, which combines 
bundles of services, components, features and IT Infrastructure 
technologies. 
Quality 
Assurance 
Agreement
Written agreement concluded between Bayer and the Supplier of 
material(s) or outsourced activities with GxP relevance. QAAs are 
not commercial or business agreements; they do not cover issues 
such as commercial terms, confidentiality, pricing, liability or

delivery issues. QAAs are separate documents, or at least

Software 
as 
a 
Service (SaaS)
The capability provided to the consumer is to use the provider’s 
applications running on a cloud infrastructure. The applications are 
accessible from various client devices through either a thin client 
interface, such as a web browser, or a program interface. The 
consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud 
infrastructure including network, servers, operating systems, 
storage, or even individual application capabilities, with the possible 
exception of limited user-specific application configuration settings.
Infrastructure as a 
Service (IaaS)
The capability provided to the consumer is to provision processing, 
storage, networks, and other fundamental computing resources 
where the consumer is able to deploy and run arbitrary software, 
which can include operating systems and applications. The 
consumer does not manage or control the underlying cloud 
infrastructure but has control over operating systems, storage, and 
deployed applications; and possibly limited control of select 
networking components (e.g., host firewalls).
Platform as a Service 
(PaaS)
The capability provided to the consumer is to deploy onto the cloud 
infrastructure consumer-created or acquired applications created 
using programming languages, libraries, services, and tools 
supported by the provider. The consumer does not manage or 
control the underlying cloud infrastructure including network, 
servers, operating systems, or storage, but has control over the 
deployed applications and possibly configuration settings for the 
application-hosting environment.
4
Purpose & Process
4.1
Purpose of managing a GxP computerized system life cycle.
Computerized systems in the GxP environment follow a controlled system life cycle from the initial 
planning to final retirement and decommissioning. This includes also the need for qualifying IT 
Infrastructure services as a prerequisite for GxP compliance of any application. The GxP computerized 
system life cycle follows a risk-based approach that takes into account risk to product quality and 
patient safety.
This document is based on external requirements listed in Section 6 and on the internal requirements, 
including those laid down in Corporate Policies for IT Governance and Information Security. 
All documents and standard operating procedures referred in the framework and further in this 
document apply according to their specific scope.
Independent of the intended area of use, an assessment of the GxP relevance (see Supplement 2071) 
is necessary for all systems in order to ensure that there has been a documented decision to validate 
a system in accordance with GSOPs 2003 – 2006 or not.
GMP/GDP systems that are closely integrated with equipment and instrumentation are alternatively 
covered in the context of qualification of the technical system as described in GSOP 125 as long as 
all applicable aspects of this procedure are covered.

metadata.titulo: Manage GxP computerized system life cycle
Manage GxP computerized system life cycle
Manage GxP computerized system life cycle

__data__.text:
Independent of the intended area of use, an assessment of the GxP relevance (see Supplement 2071) 
is necessary for all systems in order to ensure that there has been a documented decision to validate 
a system in accordance with GSOPs 2003 – 2006 or not.
GMP/GDP systems that are closely integrated with equipment and instrumentation are alternatively 
covered in the context of qualification of the technical system as described in GSOP 125 as long as 
all applicable aspects of this procedure are covered. 
When a business need for new or updated computerized systems arises, the set-up, implementation 
and release of the system need to demonstrate that the system is fit for the intended use and supports 
the business process appropriately. 
IQMS-103486 Ver. 3.0 | 2002
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-12-15
Page: 8 of 19 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-21 16:32 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

Manage GxP computerized system life cycle
RESTRICTED
After the system is released for use, the validated state of the system needs to be maintained with 
regard to changes to the system and its operating environment, until the system is finally retired. GxP-
relevant data residing in the system are managed in accordance with data integrity principles as 
described in the GSOP 257. Necessary segregation of duties related to roles assigned in the system 
is maintained.
The scope and depth of validation activities is related to system risk. In addition, the complexity of the 
system will drive the level of documentation needed.
The software categories as provided in the Section 3 “Definitions" are used in all the computerized 
system life cycle GSOPs. Hardware categories are defined in GSOP 2028. Category 1 systems are 
covered by GSOP 2028. System life cycle activities for categories 3, 4 and 5 are covered by GSOPs 
2003, 2004, 2005, and 2006.
IT security is integrated throughout the system life cycle. General IT Security Requirements are laid 
down the Policy 1435 “IT Security”, Procedure IT-MS-000249 “IT Security for Bayer-managed IT” and 
IT-MS-000250 “IT Security for IT Outsourcing and Cloud Computing”
The same principles apply independent of whether the systems are implemented, operated, and 
maintained within Bayer, at Bayer’s service providers or at a cloud provider. Nevertheless, Bayer 
remains accountable for ensuring that the system is fit for the intended use and that data integrity is 
maintained.

4.2
Roles and responsibilities
The roles and responsibilities below are the generic roles needed during the system life cycle as 
described in GSOPs 2003, 2004, 2005 and 2006. It is possible to combine roles with the exception of 
the Quality Assurance role. Where roles combined, general principles of segregation of duty still need 
to be ensured, e.g. not assigning IT Service Provider roles including admin access to applications to 
the same person as a Process Manager.

metadata.titulo: Manage GxP computerized system life cycle
Manage GxP computerized system life cycle
Manage GxP computerized system life cycle

__data__.text:
It is possible to combine roles with the exception of 
the Quality Assurance role. Where roles combined, general principles of segregation of duty still need 
to be ensured, e.g. not assigning IT Service Provider roles including admin access to applications to 
the same person as a Process Manager.
If multiple business processes or organizational units are supported by a system, then it is possible to 
assign multiple individuals to the roles below.
Roles and responsibilities are clearly defined during the complete system life cycle. If third parties are 
employed in the system life cycle, then the responsibilities are defined in contracts and Quality 
Assurance Agreements. 
Roles that are specific to IT Infrastructure Services are defined in GSOP 2028
Specific aspects for global systems are described in Annex 1.
Process Owner
-
In general, Process Ownership has two aspects

Responsibility for a business process

Responsibility for a supporting computerized system
-
In the context of this GSOP responsibility for a computerized system 
means:

Ensuring that the computerized system and its operation is fit for 
intended use and in compliance with all applicable regulations 
throughout the system life cycle.

Being accountable for the data residing in the system.

Releasing the computerized system.

Identify the Process Manager.
Process Manager
-
Takes operational responsibility for the computerized system in the 
context of the business process throughout its life cycle including:

Change Management of the computerized system.

Ensuring data validity through appropriate processes and 
procedures.

Identifying System Users and specifying their role including 
access rights.

Assuring that training material is available and System User 
training performed.

Maintaining the validated state of the computerized system.

Providing all relevant documentation for internal and external 
inspections.
In case there is no Process Manager assigned, the accountabilities during 
the process and the approvals relating to the deliverables are assigned to 
the Process Owner
System User
-
Works with the system within the business requirements and / or 
documented procedures after roll-out.
System Owner
-
Ensures:

The availability, installation, maintenance and support of the 
system during the complete system life cycle.

The security and technical data integrity of the data residing on 
that system.

Maintaining access rights as specified by the Process Manager.

Collecting and tracking errors, bugs.


Coordinating computer system changes (hardware and software).

Maintaining the validated state of the system.
The System Owner remains accountable for the activities mentioned 
above even when specific responsibilities and tasks are outsourced to a 
third-party IT Service Provider. In this case, the System Owner has to 
ensure that contracts and Quality Assurance Agreement (QAA) cover the 
necessary aspects to operate the system in accordance with this 
procedure.
IT Service Provider
-
Installs, maintains, and supports the computerized system during the 
complete system life cycle in a qualified environment.
-
Provides the IT infrastructure services necessary to run the system.
-
Proposes service levels to System Owner.
System Supplier
-
Develops the system under a quality management system.
-
Delivers the system following procedures as defined in the contracts, 
as applicable.

Quality Assurance
-
Provides regulatory guidance, independent review and approval over 
the system life cycle for compliance with relevant GxP requirements 
and procedures as described in GSOPs 2002 - 2006.
-
Performing the Supplier Quality Assessment.
-
Performing audits of IT Service Provider, System Supplier, and 
computerized systems, if applicable.
-
Releasing computerized systems.
Different aspects of the QA responsibilities can be assigned to different 
individuals in separate parts of the Quality organization.

4.3
Process flow
Description
4.3.1
Qualify IT Infrastructure Service (GSOP 2028)

Qualify IT Infrastructure Services that are used for GxP purposes based on their risk and ensure that 
the qualified state is maintained over the complete service life cycle.

Retire IT infrastructure service in a controlled manner, where services are not needed anymore 

Apply this concept for both in-house and outsourced IT Infrastructure Services.
4.3.2
Set up and implement a computerized system (GSOP 2003)

For systems that have been classified as GxP relevant, implement and test the system based on 
approved specifications and a Risk Assessment and ensure that it can be operated and maintained 
in a GxP compliant manner through procedures, training and agreements.
4.3.3
Operate and maintain a GxP computerized system (GSOP 2004)

After go-live, operate and maintain the system based on the defined procedures and agreements in 
order to ensure the validated state throughout the system life cycle.
4.3.4
Retire/decommission a computerized system (GSOP 2005)

Retire a computerized system at the end of the life cycle

Finally decommission it once it contains no data that needs to be retained from a GxP perspective, 
either because data has been discarded at the end of the record retention period or because data 
that needs to be retained has been migrated.
4.3.5
Migrate data (GSOP 2006)

Where data is migrated e.g. from one system to another, when changing storage location or 
upgrading, ensure that data remains readable, retrievable and processable to the necessary extent 
throughout the complete record retention period.

Annex 1: Roles and Responsibilities in Global Systems
For global systems, the responsibilities described in Section 4.2 apply as well, but additional 
requirements apply in order to address the accountability and responsibility split between global and 
local organizations. Where global systems are implemented, global process ownership and global 
system ownership are defined at the beginning of the process defined in this SOP. Only in cases 
where the Global Process Owner has fully accountability for the process, its implementation, validation 
and the data, no local responsibilities are defined.
Global and local roles are defined as follows:
The scope of global and local activities needs to be defined in the context of a Validation Master Plan 
during the implementation phase and in the corresponding Operational Manual applicable for the 
system during operation and maintenance phase. 
Global Process Owner
-
For complex/globally used systems these two aspects defined above for Process Ownership 
may be assigned to two different roles:
a)
Global Business Process Owner
b)
Global Process Owner IT system
-
Both of these roles are business roles. In case of a split, both roles have to closely collaborate 
to ensure consistency between the business process and the supporting computerized system
The role of the Global Business Process Owner 
-
Is in general described for GMP/GDP-related processes in GSOP 103. 
In the context of computerized systems he/she
-
Defines and approves the overall business process which serves as a basis to set up the 
Computerized system
-
Approves the GxP determination

Global Process Owner IT system (= Business Owner in the sense of Corporate Policy 2001)
-
Is accountable that an appropriate system life cycle management is in place in accordance with 
these GSOPs
-
Is accountable for defining system life cycle activities performed centrally in their area of 
responsibility
-
Is accountable for defining data ownership 
-
Ensures that Local Process Managers are identified when responsibility for system data and/or 
functionality reside in different business organizations
-
Is accountable for performing all system life cycle activities in his/her area of responsibility 
defined as coming under global scope
-
Ensures that Local Process Managers are informed about and involved at least in the definition 
of user requirements, security concepts and the risk assessment, as appropriate for the individual 
system
-
Ensures that Local Process Managers are informed about changes and enables them to address 
local impact
-
Ensures that Global Business Process Owner, Local Process Managers and Local QA are 
informed about deviations and enables them to address local impact
-
Ensures that the Local Process Managers have access to all required information and 
documentation
-
Releases the computerized system 
-
In case of changes in functionality informs and aligns with Global Business Process Owner.
Global Process Manager 
-
Is nominated by the Global Process Owner
-
Acts on behalf of the Global Process Owner in day-to-day operation of the system and is 
responsible for performing the tasks related to the GPOs accountability
-
Reviews and approves system life cycle deliverables defined as global activities
Global Quality Assurance
-
Ensures that Local Quality Assurance is informed about and involved in the validation concepts 
including their scope and changes, as appropriate for the individual system, and enables them 
to address local impact and local needs concerning validation activities
-
Performs independent review and approval of system life cycle activities coming under global 
scope
-
Performs the Supplier Quality Assessment 
-
Performs audits of IT Service Provider, System Supplier, and computerized systems, if 
applicable
-
In the GMP/GDP area, releases computerized systems
System Owner
-
See definition in Section 4.2
Local Process Manager is defined in the site-specific Validation Master Plan as defined in GSOP 
206 “Manage Validation Master Plan” and 
-
Is accountable for assuring that all GxP-relevant computerized systems used in his/her area of 
responsibility are validated.
-
Is accountable for performing all system life cycle activities defined as coming under local scope
-
Approves local Validation Plans/Protocols and Reports during implementation at the respective 
site /organizational unit. This approval includes the acceptance of the global validation approach.
-
Releases the system for local use.
Local Quality Assurance
-
Reviews and approves life cycle activities defined as coming under local scope.

Scope and Framework
1.1
Scope
This SOP defines the process and quality requirements of how to “set up and implement a 
computerized system in the GxP-regulated environment”. 
It covers the steps from defining the GxP relevance of a proposed new / updated system to go-live of 
the application in the GxP environment. 
It applies cross-functionally to all systems including relevant IT infrastructure implemented, operated,  
maintained or retired/decommissioned within the GxP regulated areas independent of whether they 
technically reside at Bayer, Bayer’s service providers or at a cloud provider (IaaS, PaaS or SaaS).

metadata.titulo: Set up and implement a computerized system in the GxP environment

Set up and implement a computerized system in the GxP environment

__data__.text:
It covers the steps from defining the GxP relevance of a proposed new / updated system to go-live of 
the application in the GxP environment. 
It applies cross-functionally to all systems including relevant IT infrastructure implemented, operated,  
maintained or retired/decommissioned within the GxP regulated areas independent of whether they 
technically reside at Bayer, Bayer’s service providers or at a cloud provider (IaaS, PaaS or SaaS).
Not in scope of this procedure are 
-
software as an integral part of a medical device or classified itself as a medical device 
-
computerized systems used within the PH GLP and GCIS areas.
-
software (systems) used in clinical studies where system specific validation SOPs in PH CDO for 
study use are available. 
-
IT Infrastructure Services, covered by GSOP2028
-
Setting up and implementing technical systems, covered by GSOP 125
1.2
Framework
This SOP describes the core process of the process group "GxP computerized system Life Cycle". 
The overarching framework is provided in the general process document GSOP 2002 "Manage GxP 
computerized system Life Cycle".
This process has interfaces with:
-
GSOP 2004: Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized system
-
GSOP 2005: Retire/Decommission a GxP-relevant computerized system 
-
GSOP 2006: Migrate a GxP-relevant computerized system
This SOP is complemented by:
-
Supplement 2071: Determination Form for GxP Relevance and System-Level Risk
-
GOI 1699: Manage GxP computerized system in projects using agile methodologies
-
GOI 1737: Manage computerized system life cycle for GxP robotic process automation
-
GOI 1861: Manage GxP computerized life cycle for systems using machine learning / artificial 
intelligence

3
Definitions
For generic definitions see SUP 200 “Glossary”.
General definitions related to computerized systems in GxP environment, including definition of 
software categories 3-5 can be found in GSOP 2002.
Configuration
(1) The arrangement of a computer system or component as defined by 
the number, nature, and interconnections of its constituent parts.
(2) In configuration management, the functional and physical 
characteristics of hardware or software as set forth in technical 
documentation or achieved in a product. (Definition from IEEE)
Machine ID
IDs used for the identification e.g. in server-to-server, application-to-
application or similar automated communication e.g. via application 
programming interfaces (APIs), so-called machine IDs are used.

__data__.text:
Configuration
(1) The arrangement of a computer system or component as defined by 
the number, nature, and interconnections of its constituent parts.
(2) In configuration management, the functional and physical 
characteristics of hardware or software as set forth in technical 
documentation or achieved in a product. (Definition from IEEE)
Machine ID
IDs used for the identification e.g. in server-to-server, application-to-
application or similar automated communication e.g. via application 
programming interfaces (APIs), so-called machine IDs are used. 
(Definition from IT-MS-000250)
Scripted Testing
Testing based on step-by-step instructions for the testers that will lead 
him/her in detail through the functionality and the outcomes to be tested
System 
specifications
Collective term for those specifications provided in general by the System 
Supplier or IT Service Provider: Functional Specification, Design 
Specification, Authorization and Role Specification, Configuration 
Specification.
Unscripted Testing
Testing based on high-level test plans describing the objective of the test 
and clear acceptance criteria. The objective is to test a complete function 
/ requirement (e.g. a specific item in the User Requirements) or to test a 
business process end-to-end. Unscripted testing requires process 
understanding and relevant knowledge (appropriate training) to judge 
whether the acceptance criteria are met.
4
Process Information
4.1
Purpose and general guidance 
The purpose of this process is to demonstrate that a computerized system is set-up and implemented, 
in a way that it is fit for the intended use and compliant with applicable GxP regulations.
All documents and standard operating procedures referred in the framework and further in this 
document apply according to their specific scope
4.1.1
Documentation requirements
Basic rules for the deliverables defined below are: 

Combining or splitting deliverables is possible as long as requirements concerning contents and 
approvals are met as given in this SOP.

Documentation is created either by the role defined in the respective section or by an appropriate 
Subject Matter Expert nominated by the person accountable for the deliverable concerned. 

Each deliverable is signed by at least two persons (authoring and approval). Approvals are 
governed by Section 4.4

The deliverables apply to all categories of software if not explicitly specified otherwise in the 
respective process steps.

Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-12-15
Page: 5 of 48 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-21 16:32 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)


Simplified documentation packages are available for certain types of systems. The types below 
are currently described in the context of the process group, but further may be defined depending 
on the area of use:

For Robotic Process Automation, the simplified approach is described in GOI 1737 “Manage 
GxP computerized system life cycle for Robotic Process Automation”.

For spread sheets, dashboards and reports a simplified documentation package is available 
using a limited number of templates that combine the relevant information for all 
deliverables.

__data__.text:
The types below 
are currently described in the context of the process group, but further may be defined depending 
on the area of use:

For Robotic Process Automation, the simplified approach is described in GOI 1737 “Manage 
GxP computerized system life cycle for Robotic Process Automation”.

For spread sheets, dashboards and reports a simplified documentation package is available 
using a limited number of templates that combine the relevant information for all 
deliverables. These are:

Best Practice 2037 “Validation Protocol for Spread Sheets / Dashboards / Reports”

Best Practice 1778 “Combined Specification – Risk Assessment – Trace Matrix”

Best Practice 1779 “UAT Protocol for Spread Sheets / Dashboards / Reports”

Best Practice 2038 “ Validation Report for Spread Sheets / Dashboards / Reports”
The management of documents and data under Bayer accountability/responsibility throughout their 
lifecycle is described in the GSOP 257 – “Manage GMP/GDP Data Lifecycle” or in PH R&D specific 
SOPs and BPDs. 
In accordance with GSOP 257:

Approved test protocols and test cases are form masters, where working forms are used for 
execution.

All test records generated in the context of validation are considered GxP records, in addition UAT 
records are considered raw data.
Software development documentation and system test documentation

Are an integral part of the supplier quality management system and Bayer is able to review it 
during supplier audits for category 3 and 4 systems.

Need to be supplied to Bayer for category 5 systems as Bayer has the ultimate responsibility for 
all aspects of the life cycle. Details about documentation contents and storage including access 
by Bayer to documentation throughout the record retention period are addressed in the 
corresponding Quality Assurance Agreement where a 3rd party develops the software.

In any case, any testing documentation used to address GxP risks and to limit User Acceptance 
Testing (see 4.1.5) is required to be available to Bayer. This documentation then also follows the 
requirements defined in GSOP 257.
4.1.2
Software Development
System Suppliers use different models for software development (e.g. agile methodologies) as 
appropriate for the specific system. Independent of the methodology used, the requirements for 
deliverables as described in section 5 apply. When projects implementing GxP computerized 
systems use agile methodologies, GOI 1699 “Manage GxP computerized system in projects using 
agile methodologies” applies.
4.1.3
Risk Assessment
Risks are assessed at several stages of the system life cycle focusing on patient safety, product quality 
and GxP data integrity by: 

Performing an initial assessment based on the business process is to identify GxP-relevance of 
the system (Determination for GxP relevance and system risk in accordance with Supplement 
2071) 

Identifying GxP-relevant process steps in the Business Process Description.

Performing a detailed assessment as part of the Risk Assessment described in section 5.5.

Reviewing performed mitigation actions in the context of system releasef and  deciding about the 
acceptability of residual risks.

Assessing risks associated with data migration in the context of GSOP 2006.



IQMS-103276 Ver. 5.0 | 2003
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-12-15
Page: 6 of 48 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-21 16:32 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

Requirements determined as not GxP relevant do not require mitigation activities (e.g. testing or 
procedural controls) in the scope of validation. 
If the Process Owner and Quality Assurance decide on a complete test of all functionality described 
in User Requirement Specification and Functional Specification, then it is acceptable to limit the Risk 
Assessment to those risks that are mitigated by procedural controls. 
Assessing risks during the computerized system life cycle is based on the general framework provided 
in GSOP 105: “Manage Quality Risk”.
4.1.4
Traceability
 Full traceability between different deliverables and the requirements is maintained:

From business process definition and associated risks to user requirements and associated risks 
and mitigations

From user requirements to system specifications 

From user requirements and associated risks, system specifications to testing or other mitigation 
actions defined during risk assessment
It is possible to document traceability by different means e.g.

The risk assessment and all traceability information combined in one document (Risk and 
Traceability Matrix).

Supported by technical solution

Embedding references directly into documents
4.1.5
Testing
4.1.5.1
Defining the test scope
Where GxP risks are covered through system design and addressed in testing, these are in general 
addressed during UAT testing. Based on the system risk (as determined in Supplement 2071) and the 
risk associated with the GxP-relevant requirements as defined in the detailed risk assessment, the 
following minimum requirements apply:
System Risk
(according to Supplement 2071)
High
Medium
Low
High-risk requirement
Scripted UAT
Scripted UAT
Unscripted UAT and / 
or Supplier Testing
Medium-risk 
requirement
Scripted UAT and / or 
Supplier Testing
Unscripted UAT and / 
or Supplier Testing
UAT not required
Low-risk requirement
Unscripted UAT and / 
or Supplier Testing
UAT not required
UAT not required
For medium and low risk combinations of functionality and system risk, it is allowed to fully replace 
scripted/unscripted UAT by supplier testing (see also section 4.2.6.2 leveraging supplier / service 
provider testing).
Combinations of scripted UAT with supplier testing to mitigate GxP risks appropriately for high risk 
functionality (in high and medium risk systems) are also possible (e.g. for CAT 5 systems).
Independent of testing being covered through scripted or unscripted testing, general requirements for 
the generation and approval of Test Protocols, documentation including evidence in the execution and 
tracking of issues encountered during testing remain the same as described in the sections below.

__data__.text:
Combinations of scripted UAT with supplier testing to mitigate GxP risks appropriately for high risk 
functionality (in high and medium risk systems) are also possible (e.g. for CAT 5 systems).
Independent of testing being covered through scripted or unscripted testing, general requirements for 
the generation and approval of Test Protocols, documentation including evidence in the execution and 
tracking of issues encountered during testing remain the same as described in the sections below.
4.1.5.2
Leveraging supplier / service provider testing
Leveraging existing supplier GxP documentation including System Testing and test results to limit 
User Acceptance Testing is allowed if the following prerequisites are fulfilled: 

the Supplier Quality Assessment is positive 
IQMS-103276 Ver. 5.0 | 2003
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-12-15
Page: 7 of 48 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-21 16:32 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)


the supplier follows good documentation practices that ensure data integrity of the information 
provided

 the supplier is able to provide the results of unit, module, integration and/or system testing and a 
defined process for releasing the system to customers

the supplier testing approach is comprehensive and well-documented, both concerning 
procedures and their implementation. This includes: 

testing documentation is structured in a way that it is possible to trace to User Requirements 
and risks

test descriptions are understandable and test executions recorded in a manner that the outcome 
of the testing is clear

testing is performed in a controlled environment to ensure that testing results are meaningful also 
for the Bayer installation. This includes being able to trace what the differences between the 
supplier’s testing environment and the final productive environment are to assess which tests 
reflect the functionality during productive use

a robust change management is in place to ensure that test results relate to the released version 
and updates are  addressing the impact of modifications on already executed tests

Process Manager, System Owner and Quality Assurance with support of the appropriate Subject 
Matter Experts have evaluated which, if any, of the supplier tests are used as part of the system 
life cycle documentation.
Executed test results are reviewed by a Subject Matter Expert appointed by the person accountable 
for the test phase. The result of the assessment is documented:

In the trace matrix as references to the test cases provided by the supplier

As part of the UAT Protocol describing the testing approach and which aspects of the supplier 
tests are used.
4.1.5.3
Test Automation Framework
Test automation follows the general framework applicable to manual testing with the following 
specific aspects

The test protocol includes a reference to the automation tools or automation services used. 
It also describes the procedures for generating the automated test scripts from the approved 
test cases and how the results are transferred back from the test tool to the documentation. 

Automation test script(s) (ATS) is the machine executable test script that contains the 
instructions for the test execution (steps to be performed, evidence to be captured, error 
handling). The ATS is created based on the test cases included in the test protocol. 

Procedures ensure that test case and ATS are equivalent. One option is to initially run the 
Test Case manually in parallel to running the generated ATS and comparing the results to 
ensure that they are identical. When alternatives are in place that provide the same level of 
assurance, they are described in the respective protocol.

metadata.titulo: Set up and implement a computerized system in the GxP environment
Set up and implement a computerized system in the GxP environment
Set up and implement a computerized system in the GxP environment

__data__.text:
The ATS is created based on the test cases included in the test protocol. 

Procedures ensure that test case and ATS are equivalent. One option is to initially run the 
Test Case manually in parallel to running the generated ATS and comparing the results to 
ensure that they are identical. When alternatives are in place that provide the same level of 
assurance, they are described in the respective protocol.

The automated test scripts are executed and any deviations, their resolution and re-runs are 
documented. 

Automated test script report provide the results of the automated test execution including 
evidence and used data values. 

The ATS reports are reviewed and approved in accordance with the requirements of this 
GSOP. 

If the automation ensures that the tests are performed successfully, then the test execution 
can be limited to the review of any exceptions that came up during the execution.
Automated Test Scripts and the results generated by the tool follow GxP documentation standards 
including requirements for data integrity and record retention.
The need for a final test report in accordance this GSOP remains unaffected.
4.1.6
Software as a Service
IQMS-103276 Ver. 5.0 | 2003
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-12-15
Page: 8 of 48 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-21 16:32 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

During the implementation phase of a new computerized system, the SaaS Provider takes the role of 
the System Supplier and the IT Service Provider where it comes to the provisioning, creation and 
approval of deliverables.
For SaaS, the requirements laid down in IT-MS-000250 “IT Security for IT Outsourcing and Cloud 
Computing” are taken into account during the implementation. 
4.1.7
Global Systems
In the beginning of the implementation phase Global Process Ownership is defined. Where a separate 
GPO is assigned for the computerized system different from the Business Process Owner, both roles 
are business roles.
The GxP Determination and Business Process description are in these cases approved by the Global 
Business Process Owner.
The Validation Master Plan and further system-specific documents are approved by the Global 
Process Owner for the IT System.

R: Responsible: Those who do the work to achieve the task. There is at least one role with a 
participation type of responsible, although others can be delegated to assist in the work required.
A: Accountable: The one ultimately answerable for the correct and thorough completion of the 
deliverable or task, and the one who delegates the work to those responsible. There must be only 
one accountable specified for each task or deliverable.
C: Consulted: Those whose opinions are sought, typically subject matter experts; and with whom 
there is two-way communication.
I: Informed: Those who are kept up-to-date on progress, often only on completion of the task or 
deliverable; and with whom there is just one-way communication.

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
5.1.3.1
GxP-relevant? No medical device?
-
If GxP relevant, establish system life cycle in accordance with GSOPs 
2002 – 2006. 
o
Follow GOI 1737 if the GxP Determination has defined the 
System Description as being Robotic Process Automation.
o
Follow in addition GOI 1861 if the system includes Artificial 
Intelligence/Machine Learning 
-
If not GxP relevant, or if the software is part of a Medical Device, this SOP 
does not apply further. For medical devices, follow GOI 2041: “Design 
Control for Medical Devices and Combination Products”
Remarks
-
For systems used in clinical studies, in case no system specific validation 
SOP for study use is available, this SOP is followed and (draft) study-
specific protocol needs to be reflected in the validation
GSOP 2002
GSOP 2004
GSOP 2005
GSOP 2006
GOI 2041
5.1.4
Create and approve Business Process Description incl. Process Risk 
Assessment
-
Detail the business processes to be supported by the computerized 
system as identified in the context of GSOP 2071 “Determination Form for 
GxP Relevance and System-Level Risk”  and identify the related GxP 
relevance for the process steps
-
Identify data that is used as input for the process steps or that is 
generated as output and identify data risk.
-
Where new or modified processes are defined, identify on a high level the 
risks associated with the process steps and associated mitigation 
measures.
Remarks
-
In some cases, only the as-is process is available at the start of a project. 
The creation and documentation of the to-be processes must then be 
done latest before the User Requirement Specification is generated.
-
Business process descriptions are either separate documents or part of 
SOPs / procedures.

-
Based on the available information, the risk associated with the supported 
business process and the level of supplier involvement into validation (e.g., 
through leveraging of supplier documentation), define further steps.
-
Evaluate if the supplier has a documented quality management system and 
objective evidence that it is followed. Qualify IT Service Providers in 
accordance with GSOP 1618 “Qualify supplier”.
-
Include the supplier into audit programs where necessary following OI 592  
“Establish Audit Program” and RD-SOP-1205 “Plan, generate and maintain 
R&D audit plan”
-
Where supplier testing documentation is planned to be leveraged, evaluate 
the provided documentation in how far it fulfills the requirements given in 
section 4.1.5.2.
Remarks
-
The assessment covers evaluation of suppliers’ quality management 
system including software lifecycle and related processes.
-
As a prerequisite for the Supplier Quality Assessment, possible solution 
suppliers are evaluated for their functional and technical capabilities to 
support the intended business use.
-
In case of SaaS or other 3rd party IT Service Providers and in addition to 
the requirements laid down in IT-MS-000250 “IT Security for IT Outsourcing 
and Cloud Computing”, the Supplier Quality Assessment needs to address 
interfaces to the customer during operation, specifically:

5.2.3
Create Validation Plan/Protocol
-
Define the required validation activities and how they will be performed 
including, at a minimum

Roles and responsibilities

Organizational and technical scope 

All deliverables for the validation in scope

General acceptance criteria for validation completion

Timelines, if applicable
-
Use the “Software Description” of Section 8.2.2 to determine the socpe and 
extent of validation activities, if not yet done in the context of the project-
specific Validation Master Plan. Add more detail on the overall software 
architecture if necessary (e.g. parts that are in-house developed vs. 
configurable parts)
Remarks
-
The Process Owner is accountable if no project-specific Validation Master 
Plan is available; otherwise accountability resides with the Process 
Manager.
-
Categorization follows the software categories as given in GSOP 2002 for 
software. In some cases multiple systems and interfaces are used to 
support a business solution. These need to be categorized separately. 
Categorization of infrastructure is not part of this documentation.
-
A template is provided in Best Practice 2009: “Template for Validation 
Plan/Protocol”.
-
For category 3 systems, it is permitted to create the Validation 
Plan/Protocol at a later stage in combination with respective test protocols.
-
The Best Practice 2037 “Template – Excel and Individual Report Validation 
Plan” is provided for validation of spread sheets and for dashboards reports 
generated from e.g. data warehouses or data lakes.
-
Best Practice 2057 “Template for Validation Plan Protocol for local roll-outs 
of global systems” is provided for global systems. During local roll-outs 
these protocols are approved by both global and local Process Managers 
and QA to ensure local acceptance and consistency with the global 
validation approach.

Exceptions to this rule are

PO or PM, SO, QA
5.2.5
Create User Requirements Specification 
-
Describe WHAT the computerized system is supposed to do from a System 
User’s point of view, independent of a specific solution. The functionality 
must be in accordance with and linked to the business process.

Address the following items:

Functional requirements including data input, processing, and reporting

Data handling requirements including data entry (including, where 
necessary, contemporaneous saving), data modifications, data 
deletions, data processing 

Electronic records requirements including audit trail requirements for 
data generation, modification and deletion and audit trail review 
requirements, and electronic signatures (if applicable), 

Data definitions and data flow including definition of which data are 
considered raw or source data together with a definition of the data to be 
considered as electronic record(s) including original data or true copies, 
and whether data needs to be available in dynamic or static format.

Performance requirements including data volume

Interface requirements including system user, other software and/or 
interfaces specific hardware, where necessary (e.g. associated specific 
scanners or label printers) 

Availability requirements including uninterrupted utilization period and 
back-up and restore requirements.
-
Security requirements including roles, accessibility of system functions 
and data (including audit trails), see also section on the Business Role 
Description. These include also requirements derived from Procedure 
IT-MS-000249 “IT Security for Bayer-managed IT” and Procedure IT-
MS-000250 “IT Security for outsourced IT” such as the use of unique 
user IDs throughout the system.

Maintenance requirements

GxP, quality and legal requirements including record retention 
requirements

Environmental and physical requirements, if applicable

Constraints to be observed
Remarks
-
A template is provided in Best Practice 2008: “Template for User 
Requirements Specification”
PM
IT-MS-000249
IT-MS-000250
Best Practice 2008

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
-
The following applies to all requirements within the User Requirements 
Specification:

Each requirement is complete, feasible, clearly stated, and specific 
enough for testing. 

Requirements do not contradict each other.

Each requirement is uniquely identifiable to permit traceability (e.g. 
alphanumeric). Dependencies between requirements are clearly 
indicated.
-
For Spread Sheets / Reports and Dashboards a simplified approach is 
provided in Best Practice 1778: “Template for Combined Specification / 
Risk Assessment / Traceability” including all necessary specifications.
Best Practice 1778

5.3
Develop system
Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
5.3.1
Create and approve Design Specification
-
Describe the detailed design of functions of the system in the Design 
Specification as documented in the Functional Specification from a system 
developer’s point of view. These include, but are not limited to:

Definition of required systems functions, modes of operation and behavior 
such as
o
Main functions and sub-functions
o
Assumptions, dependencies & constraints
o
Inputs: e.g. critical data entry including data changes and audit trails
o
Outputs: e.g. reports
o
Calculations
o
Error detection and recovery
o
Memory management policies
o
Operational system checks
o
Data handling requirements
o
Electronic signature implementation
o
Interfaces / Interactions with other computerized systems / 
equipment
o
Modes of operation (start-up, normal, shutdown, test, fallback)
o
Performance of the system and transaction priority
o
Data security like access authorization and password requirements
o
Archiving (where applicable): Definition of data that will be stored 
along with retrieval requirements
-
Approve the Design Specification at the end of this step
Remarks

5.3.3
Design unit/module tests
-
Test in the Module testing individual software components or groups of 
related components. This means

With modular computerized systems, the program modules are tested 
during program compilation. The specifications for testing are derived from 
the software module design specifications for the appropriate modules.

Module test protocols include
o
Test scope
o
Prerequisites
o
Testing conditions
o
Test environment
o
Test procedures to be employed
o
General acceptance criteria
o
Test cases to be executed

Individual test steps to be executed
o
Expected results

and Best Practice 2014: “Template for Test Case”.

5.3.4
Execute unit/module tests
-
Execute as test the test cases as described in the Unit / Module Test 
Protocol. Documentation includes

Test run number

Actual results

Any deviations from the expected results

Statement about the success of testing for each step (pass/fail)

Statement about the overall success of the test

Who performed the tests and when
-
Document as tester test execution contemporaneously and sign executed test 
cases.
-
Check and sign as reviewer for the completeness and appropriateness of all 
entries and the fact that all deviations from the expected results were 
appropriately addressed. 
-
Remark:

5.4

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
5.4.1.1
Configurable?
-
If the software is configurable, continue with 5.4.2
-
In any case perform step 5.4.4 and following
5.4.2
Create Configuration Specification 
-
Record all configuration settings, whether default or customized for 
Category 4 and configurable Category 5 software. This includes:

Identifying and implementing of configuration items that affect system 
functionality, security and data integrity.

A description of settings in the system that are used as master data.
-
Ensure that system configurations are set-up to support data integrity, e.g. 
required audit trails are always turned on.
Remarks
-
The Configuration Specification serves as a description of how to retrieve 
configuration setting from the computerized system if configuration settings 
are accessible, retrievable and traceable including audit trails for the 
settings. 
-
The initial Configuration Specification is provided by the System Supplier. 
Following versions of the Configuration Specification are maintained either 
by the System Supplier or by the IT Service Provider depending on 
assigned responsibilities. 
-
A template is provided in Best Practice 1780 “Template for Configuration 
Specification”

5.5
Assess risk and install system
Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
5.5.1
Create Risk Assessment (incl. Fit/Gap Analysis)
-
Verify whether the specifications are acceptable and cover the intended use 
of this computerized system as described in the User Requirements 
Specifications including any necessary data integrity aspects (fit/gap analysis, 
Design Qualification). 
-
Use as a foundation the information of the Risk Assessment based on the 
business process as provided in section 5.1.4 and the system risk defined in 
Supplement 2071 “
-
Identify, evaluate and reduce risks that might negatively impact patient safety, 
product quality, and data integrity. This means

Evaluate user requirements to identify GxP-relevant functionality and 
associated risks.

Evaluate the requirements related to the data required by user 
requirements as inputs or outputs of the process steps 
o
Evaluate system specifications to identify risks to data in the system 
and required data integrity
o
Identify through the system specifications available mitigations 
through system design for physical data security, system access 
security, critical system configurations including date / time, roles, 
and 
o
Evaluate audit trails that need to be reviewed including frequency. 

Identify for the GxP-relevant/data integrity-relevant risks possible hazards 
and their impact 

Based on the above, define the mitigation actions that are applicable for 
each risk. 
a)
Verify the system design through testing based on the requirements 
in section 4.1.5.1. In this context, define the approach for regression 
testing to be used in later stages of the life cycle based on GSOP 
2004 
b)
Focus organizational mitigation actions (e.g., organizational 
segregation of duties, procedures, training) on those steps where a 
detailed risk assessment based on the table below is required.

A roll-back strategy

accordance with GSOP 2028: “IT Infrastructure Service Life Cycle 
Management”.

ensured as part of the computerized system validation.

5.6.10
Create User Acceptance Test Protocol 
-
Plan and perform testing conducted on a complete, integrated system to 
evaluate the system’s compliance with its specified requirements. 
-
Verify system functionality based on the defined business processes and the 
user requirements and the risk assessment. Test Protocols include, but are 
not limited to:

Test scope

Prerequisites

Testing conditions

Test environment

Test procedures to be employed

General acceptance criteria

Test cases to be executed
-
Include into the individual test cases information about:

Purpose

Prerequisites

Necessary data and authorizations

Testing conditions, if applicable

Individual test steps to be executed

Expected results
-
Base scope and extent of testing on the outcome of the Risk Assessment.
-
Describe in the Protocol whether supplier testing including System Tests are 
leveraged and explain risks that are mitigated through leveraged tests.

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
5.7.1
Provide System Manual
-
Describe all technical operations which are carried out by the IT Service 
Provider for the routine operation of the computerized system. The System 
Manual contains the following items, as far as applicable for the individual 
system:

System environment (hardware, operating system and further software)

Description of the controlled start-up and shut-down of the system

Recovery (how the system is returned to a defined condition after 
uncontrolled failure)

Technical user management and granting and revoking access rights

Administration of master data / files (updating of files, setting default 
values, configuration of interfaces)

Description of access to and evaluation possibilities for audit trails.

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference

System management and maintenance, e.g. 
o
Database reorganization
o
Generation of export files for data backup
o
Archiving, retrieval of archived data
o
Monitoring computerized system (utilization, error signals etc.)
o
Maintenance
Remarks
-
For category 3 systems this document is usually combined with the User 
Manual and delivered as one document by the System Supplier.
5.7.2
Approve System Manual
Review and approve the System Manual as provided by the System Supplier
Remarks:
-
The System Supplier has a formalized process within his Quality 
Management System to release Functional Specifications to the customer
IT SP
5.7.3
Ensure availability of system operation procedures
-
Ensure that after go-live the system can be maintained in a validated state. 
-
Before go-live, review the following processes required in GSOP 2004 and 
create or update related procedures, as necessary:

Roles and responsibilities for the operation and maintenance phase

Access rights management

Incident and problem management (system support)

Deviation management

Change management including approach for verifying changes to system 
settings and configuration.

Periodic review 

Periodic review of system audit trails as defined in the risk assessment.

Business continuity

Documentation management

Back-up and restore

Data archiving and retrieval
-
Ensure that mitigation actions defined in the Risk Assessment related to 
system operation procedures are addressed.
Remarks
-
Approvals are done in accordance with the respective approval processes for 
procedures (e.g. SOPs, working instructions, etc.).
-
For global systems the Operational Manual encompasses roles and 
responsibilities and details necessary and is used in conjunction with the 
provision of GSOP 2004 “Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized 
system”.
-
For the periodic review consider the following topics: (input, processing, 
change, deletion, disposal, output, paper-print out) related with the system.
SO, PM
5.7.4
Create and approve User Manual
PM

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference

Create a user manual that is understandable for the intended audience 
(system users) of the system, describing the functions and the operation of 
the system. The User Manual contains the following items, but not limited to:

Glossary

General description of the system

Starting the system

System operation (e.g. description of standard screens, keyboard 
shortcuts, roles, description of functionality including functionality for 
accessing audit trails, where necessary, etc.)

Error messages with possible reactions

Contact information for support
-
Approve the User Manual
Remarks
-
For category 3 systems under normal circumstances the User Manuals as 
provided by the System Supplier are considered sufficient. In this case no 
explicit approval is required.
5.7.5
Ensure creation of system use procedures
-
Review mitigation actions from the Risk Assessment related to:

roles and responsibilities of users, 

instructions for changing critical data, metadata and master data, and 

reviewing and approving data (including routine audit trail reviews), where 
relevant for the business process
and include relevant aspects into system-use procedures to ensure that after 
go-live the system can be maintained in a validated state.
Remarks
-
Approvals are done in accordance with the respective approval processes for 
procedures (e.g. SOPs, best practice documents, working instructions, etc.).
-
For the data review please consider the following topics: input, processing, 
change, deletion, disposal, output, paper-print out 
PM 
5.7.6
Create and approve Training Concept

Create and approve a Training Concept to ensure that the employees 
responsible for using and operating the system for its intended use can 
perform their respective functions properly.
-
Include a definition of necessary level of training for each role in the system 
(including System Support roles)
-
Define necessary training material and trainer qualification
PM 
5.7.7
Ensure availability of Training Material
-
Ensure that Training Material is available so that employees responsible for 
using and operating the system for its intended use can perform their 
respective functions properly. 
-
Ensure that mitigation actions defined in the Risk Assessment  related to 
training are covered in the training material.
PM 
5.7.8
Ensure support personnel training
SO

-

5.7.10 Decide on outsourcing support services
-
Decide on the future support organization and whether services are 
outsourced to 3rd party service providers.
SO, PM
5.7.10.1 3rd party service provider used?
-
If the answer is yes, complete step 5.7.11
-
If the answer is no, the process ends.
5.7.11 Ensure availability of QAA
-
Ensure that after go-live the system is maintained in a validated state when 
operation and maintenance processes are outsourced to 3rd party IT Service 
Providers. 
-
Define in Quality Assurance Agreements (QAA) relevant aspects for activities 
described in 5.3.11 that are outsourced. For details see GSOP 2004.
Remarks
-
A template is provided in Best Practice 2035 – “Template for Quality 
Assurance Agreement for Computerized Systems”
-
The general framework for managing Quality Assurance Agreements is 
provided in GSOP 500 "Manage Quality Assurance Agreements". GSOP 
1618 “Qualify supplier” defines the need for Quality Assurance Agreements 
based on system risk.

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference

Create Validation Report
-
Summarize validation results, system acceptance and release to 
production
-
Review and evaluate all system life cycle deliverables including data 
migration results. The report states

What was done
o
The results stating the fulfilment of acceptance criteria
o
Any special considerations regarding the use of the system that 
were identified 
o
Any deviations found and conclusions drawn
o
Recommendation of acceptance and release
-
Review in the context of system release, performed mitigation actions 
based on the Risk Assessment and decide about the acceptability of 
residual risks.
Remarks
-
A template is provided in Best Practice 2016 “Template for Validation 
Report”.

__data__.text:
It covers the activities from go-live of a GxP-relevant system until the decision is taken for retirement.
It applies cross-functionally to all systems including relevant IT infrastructure implemented, operated, 
maintained or retired/decommissioned within the GxP regulated areas independent of whether they 
technically reside at Bayer, Bayer’s service providers or at a cloud provider (Infrastructure as a Service, 
Platforms as a Service, Software as a Service).
Not in scope of this procedure are 
-
software as an integral part of a medical device or classified itself as a medical device 
-
computerized systems used within the PH GLP and GCIS areas.
-
software (systems) used in clinical studies where system specific validation SOPs in PH CDO for 
study use are available. 
-
IT Infrastructure Services, covered by GSOP2028
-
Setting up and implementing technical systems, covered by GSOP 125
1.2
Framework
This GSOP describes the core process of the process group "Manage a GxP computerized system 
life cycle". 
The overarching framework is provided in the general process document GSOP 2002 "Manage a GxP 
computerized system life cycle ".
This process has interfaces with:
-
GSOP 2003: Set up and implement a computerized system in the GxP environment
-
GSOP 2005: Retire/Decommission a GxP-relevant computerized system 
-
GSOP 2006: Migrate a GxP-relevant computerized System 
This SOP is complemented by:
-
Supplement 2074: Template for Operational Manual for Global Systems
-
GOI 1270: SAP Authorization Management (SAM)
-
GOI 1737: Manage computerized system life cycle for GxP robotic process automation
-
GOI 1775: Manage identity and access for GxP relevant systems with Bayer IdentityNow
-
GOI 1861: Manage GxP computerized life cycle for systems using machine learning / artificial 
intelligence

3
Definitions
For generic definitions see Supplement 200 “Glossary”.
General definitions related to computerized systems in GxP environment, including definition of 
software categories 3-5 can be found in GSOP 2002.
Configuration
(1) The arrangement of a computer system or component as defined by 
the number, nature, and interconnections of its constituent parts.
(2) In configuration management, the functional and physical 
characteristics of hardware or software as set forth in technical 
documentation or achieved in a product. (Definition from IEEE) System 
configuration impacts on system appearance, system funcionality or 
system behaviour.
Configuration 
Management
A discipline applying technical and administrative direction and 
surveillance to identify and document the functional and physical 
characteristics of a configuration item, control changes to those 
characteristics, record and report change processing and implementation 
status, and verify compliance with specified requirements.
Data backup
True Copy of current data, metadata and system configuration settings 
(e.g.

metadata.titulo: Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized system
Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized system
Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized system

__data__.text:
(Definition from IEEE) System 
configuration impacts on system appearance, system funcionality or 
system behaviour.
Configuration 
Management
A discipline applying technical and administrative direction and 
surveillance to identify and document the functional and physical 
characteristics of a configuration item, control changes to those 
characteristics, record and report change processing and implementation 
status, and verify compliance with specified requirements.
Data backup
True Copy of current data, metadata and system configuration settings 
(e.g. variable settings which relate to an analytical run) maintained for the 
purpose of recovery (including disaster recovery). Data backup shall not 
be confused with data archiving. Data backup does not substitute data 
archive.
Incident
An unplanned interruption to an IT Service or reduction in the Quality of 
an IT Service including any event which could affect an IT Service in the 
future. For example: Failure of one disk from a mirror set. (Definition from 
ITIL)
Performance 
Monitoring 
The process responsible for day-to-day capacity management activities, 
including monitoring, threshold detection, performance analysis and 
tuning, and implementing changes related to performance and capacity.
Problem
The root cause of one or more incidents. (Definition from ITIL)
If an incident has impact on data availability or integrity and / or on the 
functionality of the system, the incident is systematically handled as a 
problem and will be handled within Problem Management in order to 
identify the root cause of incidents and to prevent recurrence
Problem 
Management
All activities required to diagnose the incidents identified through the 
Incident Management process, and to determine the resolution to those 
problems. 
Recovery
The restoration of a system, program, database or other system resource 
to a state in which it can perform required functions.
Restore
Returning a configuration item or an IT service to a working state. This 
starts with recovery of an IT service including recovering data to a known 
consistent state. After recovery it is decided whether further steps are 
needed before the IT service can be made available to the users.
System 
Administration
The routine management and support of systems to ensure that they are 
running efficiently and effectively.
True Copy
A copy of the original record that has been verified and may be retained 
in the same or a different format in which it was originally generated, e.g. 
a paper copy of a paper record, an electronic scan of a paper record, or 
a paper record of electronically generated data

4
Process Information
4.1
Purpose and general guidance 
Once a system has been released for use in the context of a business process (productive use), 
controls and procedures must be in place to ensure that the system remains fit for the intended 
purpose and in a validated state over the complete life cycle.
All process steps defined in this GSOP apply to system categories 3, 4 and 5 as described in GSOP 
2002.
All documents and standard operating procedures referred to in the framework section herein and 
within this document apply according to their specific scope.
4.1.1
Documentation
It is possible to combine or split deliverables as necessary as long as requirements concerning 
contents and approvals are met as given in the GSOPs.
Required documentation is created either by the role defined as accountable below or by an 
appropriate Subject Matter Expert for the deliverable concerned.
For more details on general documentation requirements see GSOP 2003.

metadata.titulo: Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized system
Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized system
Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized system

__data__.text:
All documents and standard operating procedures referred to in the framework section herein and 
within this document apply according to their specific scope.
4.1.1
Documentation
It is possible to combine or split deliverables as necessary as long as requirements concerning 
contents and approvals are met as given in the GSOPs.
Required documentation is created either by the role defined as accountable below or by an 
appropriate Subject Matter Expert for the deliverable concerned.
For more details on general documentation requirements see GSOP 2003.
4.1.2
Change Management
The approach in section 5.4 encompasses all changes to the computerized system including its related 
procedures that have not been explicitly excluded by the exceptions below.
IT Infrastructure:
-
IT Infrastructure components necessary for the computerized system are initially assessed for 
impact on data integrity, consistency or quality, as well as system functionality, performance or 
stability. The assessment is documented in the System Overview (see GSOP 2003). Changes to 
these infrastructure components are assessed as part of the change management process for the 
computerized system.
-
Changes to IT Infrastructure with no impact as defined above follow exclusively GSOP 2028. 
Abbreviated changes: 
-
It is possible to define an abbreviated Change Management process for minor changes to the 
application with known impact and low risk to data integrity and system functionality. 
-
Process Manager, System Owner and Quality Assurance approve the types of changes coming 
in scope of this process including necessary approvals and documentation requirements (e.g. for 
testing). 
Emergency Changes:
-
If external regulations or internal procedures define a business process as critical, then the system 
requires a process for Emergency Changes. Specific aspects are defined in the system-specific 
procedures.
-
An Emergency Change is a remediation for system errors that cannot be postponed and must be 
implemented immediately to prevent critical interruption of the supported business processes 
because the error prevents the user to fulfill a critical task within the required timeline. The 
definition of emergencies is either pre-defined or on a case-by-case basis.
-
Emergency Change are approved before implementation. 
-
In the case of emergencies, it is permitted to implement changes before finalizing all deliverables 
required by the change. Nevertheless, documented testing needs to be performed before 
productive use. 
-
The required documentation is then completed within 10 working days after change approval. 
-
Closure of the change needs to be approved by Process Manager, System Owner and Quality 
Assurance.

-
If more than 10 days are needed, an impact assessment is documented and approved by Process 
Manager, System Owner and Quality Assurance. The documentation is closed within the time 
frame defined in the impact assessment.
Change to Master Data: 
Master data are business data and do not come under the change process in this section. Master data 
management is part of the procedures under responsibility of the respective Process Owner/Process 
Manager. 
Change Management Boards
For global systems, Change Management Boards (also called Change Advisory Boards) are 
established in order to allow for input from and involvement of all relevant Global and Local Process 
Managers as well as from the System Owner and Quality Assurance.

__data__.text:
The documentation is closed within the time 
frame defined in the impact assessment.
Change to Master Data: 
Master data are business data and do not come under the change process in this section. Master data 
management is part of the procedures under responsibility of the respective Process Owner/Process 
Manager. 
Change Management Boards
For global systems, Change Management Boards (also called Change Advisory Boards) are 
established in order to allow for input from and involvement of all relevant Global and Local Process 
Managers as well as from the System Owner and Quality Assurance. 
Change Management Boards 
-
Meet on a regular basis as defined in the Operational Manual
-
Decide about upcoming changes to a global system 
-
Address any concerns brought up by the Local Process Managers
-
Review changes to the Operational Manual
-
Review system-related deviations
-
Review the outcome any periodic review activities
Approvals of Change Requests are performed by the global roles for all changes with impact on more 
than one site or on global processes. Changes with impact on one site are either approved by the 
Local Process Managers and the Local Quality Assurance or the global responsible persons, 
depending on the procedures laid down in the Operational Manual.
4.1.3
Operational Manuals for Global Systems
For the operation and maintenance phase an Operational Manual is required for global systems. The 
document contains:
-
Specific roles and responsibilities for the system
-
The activity split with System Owners at 3rd party service providers where part of the System 
Owner responsibilities have been outsourced.
-
Defining activities coming under local or global scope
-
Defining scope and extent of global and local periodic review activities
-
Procedures for information of Local Process Managers/Local QA in the case of relevant changes 
or major deviations.
-
Involvement of Local Process Managers into the Change Management Boards (see below)
-
Procedural details based on the processes described in the GSOP (e.g. when the abbreviated 
Change Management process is used)
-
Specific points to be considered during the roll-out of the system to new sites/organizational units
The template is provided in Supplement 2074 “Template for Operational Manual for Global Systems”.
If Operational Manuals are updated, the Global Process Manager ensures that Local Process 
Managers are informed.
For local systems, it can be appropriate to generate an Operational Manual considering respective 
system-specific aspects detailing requirements of the GSOP.

A: Accountable: The one ultimately answerable for the correct and thorough completion of the 
deliverable or task, and the one who delegates the work to those responsible. There must be only one 
accountable specified for each task or deliverable.
C: Consulted: Those whose opinions are sought, typically subject matter experts; and with whom 
there is two-way communication.
I: Informed: Those who are kept up to date on progress, often only on completion of the task or 
deliverable; and with whom there is just one-way communication.

If existing, verify that all necessary aspects are

Remarks

A template is provided in Best Practice 2035: “Template for Quality 
Assurance Agreement for Computerized Systems”.

IT Infrastructure used follows the related procedure GSOP 2028 “IT 
Infrastructure Service Life Cycle Management”.

Where no QAA with the Service Provider can be signed for high and 
medium risk systems, a risk assessment approved by QA needs to be 
provided describing the alternative approach. In any case a clear 
description of the provided services / service levels needs to be provided 
in the contracts.

Where applications are outsourced to a Cloud provider (SaaS), Quality 
Assurance Agreements need to include in detail: 

Detailed roles and responsibilities including interfaces and 
communication channels and responsibilities for documentation.

Expected documentation for the system.

Change Management process including Bayer involvement into the 
change process and required documentation.

Incident & problem management including Bayer notification of 
incidents and problems with potential GxP impact.

Security management / data integrity requirements including access to 
data, separation of data from other customers, data storage location 
(where necessary).

Data archiving/record retention requirements including the need to 
retain data for specified periods and ensuring availability and legibility 
of data even in cases the SaaS provider will discontinue the service.

GxP-relevant data or functionality potentially affected?


PM (A), SO, QA
5.2.7
Manage Deviation

Follow for deviation management as described in GSOP 114. 

Consider specific role assignments for computerized systems:

Create the initial Deviation Report, including an initial assessment of 
the deviation and its impact on GxP-related data and system use and 
proposing necessary immediate remediation and corrective and 
preventive actions. 

Perform initial classification.

Inform further affected functions without undue delay about the 
concerned deviation.

Initiate change management if a system change needs to be performed as 
a CAPA.
Remarks

A template is provided in Best Practice 2017: Template for Deviation 
Report incl. Closure

In case where all necessary information for a final assessment is available, 
it is possible to close out the deviation itself before all corrective and 
preventive measures are closed.

For global systems, the Global Process Manager and the Global Quality 
Assurance ensure that "major" and "critical" deviations are communicated 
GSOP 114
Best Practice 2017

Periodic review of access rights
Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
5.3.2.1
Perform access rights review

Review based on risk GxP relevant access rights for a system on a regular 
basis to ensure that only authorized persons have access to the system. This 
means reviewing roles for System Users concerning:
a)
Adherence to procedures for assignment, changes and withdrawal of 
access rights including segregation of duties. 
b)
Appropriate level of access for the users in the system.

Define the frequency and scope of this review based on the Risk Assessment 
as described in GSOP 2003, which also identifies the GxP criticality of the 
roles. Read-only roles are excluded from the review.

5.5
Manage business continuity for a GxP IT system
Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
5.5.1
Manage business continuity for a GxP IT System

Maintain a Business Continuity Plan for a business process including 
associated computerized systems.  

Include contingency planning for the case when the computerized system 
is unavailable. Include at a minimum high and medium risk systems into 
Business Continuity Planning.

Specify depending on the risk of the system:
-
Affected employees and responsible persons to be informed. 
-
How long the computerized system can be unavailable before the 
contingency plan is exercised. 
-
The business process to be followed until the system becomes 
available again. 
-
The strategy to bring the data created offline into the system and how 
long it takes to bring it in. 
-
Testing of the alternative business process 
-
Measures needed to ensure that the system is functional and data 
integrity has been preserved when the system is brought back into 
operation.
Remarks

A template is provided in Best Practice 2036 – Template for Business 
Continuity Plan for Computerized Systems

Technical processes for bringing the system back into use after severe 
hardware and software failures, fire, flood, lightning strikes, security 
breaches and employee absenteeism (Disaster Recovery) are part of the 
IT Service Providers procedures.  Quality-related business requirements 
e.g. priorities for bringing a system back into use need to be defined in the 
Quality Assurance Agreement / SOPs.
PO (A), SO
