Manage back-up and restore

Perform regular back-ups of all relevant data. 

Define requirements for contents and frequency of backups following a risk 
based approach. Define frequencies for backup in a way that they are 
sufficient to ensure that data critical to GxP operations is not lost (e.g. at 
least daily).

Ensure that the requirements are appropriately implemented.

Test and monitor the integrity and accuracy of backup data and the ability 
to restore the data periodically or if

 The back-up process or the back-up tools changes

the restore process or the restore tools change

If the overall IT system architecture (infrastructure) has been changed 
substantially (e.g., migration from on-premise to Cloud, change of 
database from SQL to Oracle,etc.



change dates for data etc. where these contain information relevant for the 
review. The definition of what information is reviewed depends on the

Remarks:

There are two types of audit trail reviews: 

Routine/Operative audit trail reviews: These reviews are part of routine 
system use and integrated e.g., into the review of batch 
documentation. The Routine/Operative audit trail reviews are covered 
in GSOP 257

Periodic/System audit trail reviews: These reviews are performed on a 
time or event driven basis

For systems with limited functionality and audit trails, a detailed risk 
analysis of individual functionality may not add value. In these cases, all 
relevant audit trails would be assessed as high-risk in the system context.
GSOP 257
5.9.2
Create periodic audit trail review report

Create a report that covers the following topics:

Description of the audit trail entries being reviewed.

Description of how these actions are reproducibly retrieved from the 
system. Attach the full list of audit trails in case such an approach is 
not possible.

Evaluation of the actions and if they were appropriate and followed 
procedures (e.g. change in critical system configuration was 
completed with a change control record).

List of any audit trail records that require investigation, defined action 
items including tracking of those.

Summary of the review


For system reviews that are initiated as a result of a deviation, define the 
scope of the system review.
Remarks

For systems supporting more than one business process, it is permitted to 
define different periodic review frequencies per process where the system 
architecture and documentation structure allow such separation.

In addition, the Process Manager, System Owner and QA evaluate 
whether a for-cause Periodic Reviews is necessary e.g. as a consequence 
of deviations indicating systematic issues that require a root-cause 
analysis (e.g. after a system breakdown with extensive restoration of 
programs and electronic records or in case of system failures or data 
integrity issues with direct impact on product quality or patient safety). 

In the case of for-cause reviews not all aspects of the system life cycle 
documentation and procedure need to be reviewed in every case. If no full 
review is performed, the scope and rationale is defined in the respective 
Periodic Review Protocol
5.10.1.1 Full review?



5.10.4
Execute system periodic review

Evaluate whether the system is still in a validated state considering the 
following points:
-
Whether documentation is up to date.
-
Impact of changes carried out since last validation on the validity of the 
system and its associated data.
-
Whether reported problems and deviations have an overall impact on 
the intended function and use of the system.
-
Whether procedures and agreements for system use, maintenance 
and operation are up to date.

Retire and decommission a GxP-relevant computerized system
RESTRICTED
1
Scope and Framework
1.1
Scope
This GSOP defines the process and quality requirements of how to “Retire and decommission a GxP-
relevant computerized system”. 
It covers the steps from deciding to retire a GxP-relevant computerized system supporting regulated 
processes to final decommissioning. 
It applies cross-functionally to all systems including relevant IT infrastructure implemented, operated ,  
maintained or retired/decommissioned within the GxP regulated areas independent of whether they 
technically reside at Bayer, Bayer’s service providers or at a cloud provider (IaaS, PaaS or SaaS).
Not in scope of this procedure is software as an integral part of a medical device or classified itself as 
a medical device as well as computerized systems used within the PH GLP and GCIS areas.
Out of scope are in addition
-
IT Infrastructure Services, covered by GSOP2028
-
Decommissioning of technical systems, covered by GSOP 125 and GOI 1413
1.2
Framework
This SOP describes one core process of the process group “Retire and decommission a GxP-relevant 
computerized system”. 
The overarching framework is provided in the general process document GSOP 2002 “Manage GxP 
computerized system life cycle”.
This process has interfaces with:
-
GSOP 2003: Set up and implement a computerized system in the GxP environment
-
GSOP 2004: Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized system 
-
GSOP 2006: Migrate a GxP-relevant computerized system
2
Abbreviations
PO
Process Owner
SO
System Owner
QA
Quality Assurance
IaaS
Infrastructure as a Service
PaaS
Platform as a Service
SaaS
Software as a Service
3
Definitions
For generic definitions see SUP 200 “Glossary”.
General definitions related to computerized systems in GxP environment, including definition of 
software categories 3-5 can be found in GSOP 2002.
System retirement 
means taking a computerized system out of active use, but still 
maintaining it including any necessary data for retrieval purposes
System 
decommissioning
When a system is decommissioned, then all associated data, software 
and hardware is removed and not available anymore for later 
restoration.
IQMS-36112 Ver. 4.0 | 2005
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2021-10-15
Page: 4 of 12 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-21 16:33 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

Retire and decommission a GxP-relevant computerized system
RESTRICTED
4
Process Information
4.1
Purpose and general guidance
The retirement and decommissioning of a computerized system is a formal act of discontinuing a 
system, marking the end of the system life cycle.

metadata.titulo: Retire and decommission a GxP-relevant computerized system
Retire and decommission a GxP-relevant computerized system
Retire and decommission a GxP-relevant computerized system

__data__.text:
System retirement happens for a variety of reasons, including:

Outsourcing or legal transfer of business operations to another organization or company

Replacing the existing system

Discontinuing a business operation
The potential impact on existing systems, data and processes is examined prior to retirement and 
decommissioning. The process ensures that there is no disruption in GxP-relevant processes or 
process steps and that data integrity is retained over the data life cycle.
Depending on the approach chosen for a specific system, both steps are either performed at the same 
point in time or as separate exercises. If the steps are combined; then the process below will have the 
steps for retirement and decommissioning combined.
If data migration is necessary, GSOP 2006 applies. System decommissioning can take place as soon 
as the data available in the system is not needed anymore, either because the end of the record 
retention period has been reached or because all relevant data has been migrated or archived.
The requirements of this document apply to software categories (3, 4 and 5).
All documents and standard operating procedures referred in the framework and further in this 
document apply according to their specific scope.
It is possible to combine or split deliverables as necessary, as long as requirements concerning 
contents and approvals are met as given in the GSOPs 
Required documentation is created either by the role defined as accountable below or by an 
appropriate Subject Matter Expert for the deliverable concerned.

R: Responsible: Those who do the work to achieve the task. There is at least one role with a 
participation type of responsible, although others can be delegated to assist in the work required.
A: Accountable: The one ultimately answerable for the correct and thorough completion of the 
deliverable or task, and the one who delegates the work to those responsible. There must be only one 
accountable specified for each task or deliverable.
C: Consulted: Those whose opinions are sought, typically subject matter experts; and with whom 
there is two-way communication.
I: Informed: Those who are kept up-to-date on progress, often only on completion of the task or 
deliverable; and with whom there is just one-way communication.

Decide on retirement and data handling 

Decide on retiring and / or decommissioning a computerized system and 
document the decision in a Change Request in accordance with GSOP

retained in the system is accessible for the time specified by the most 
stringent applicable GxP and / or legal requirements in effect regardless of 
the retirement of the system.

legal aspects have to be taken into account, especially when a decision to 
discard data is taken.

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
h)
Existing documentation relating to the system to be retired 
including:

Business process documentation

System use and support procedures

Training materials and records

User manuals

Contracts e.g. Quality Assurance Agreements (QAA) 

System interface documentation

Open change requests and deviations

Validation / qualification documentation

Source code (for category 5)

Decisions about infrastructure used including hardware in order to 
ensure that data for decommissioned systems does not remain 
readable to unintended parties, in lines with the requirements of Bayer 
Policy 1435 “Information Security”

Deliverables

Schedule.

Evaluate whether the following parties need to be involved:

Legal, especially when a decision to discard data is taken.

IT Service Provider, if it is necessary to ensure that retired systems are 
still supported with data retrieval capabilities are in place.

Other organizational functions, e.g. Regulatory Affairs, depending on 
the scope of the system.
Remarks

A template is provided in Best Practice 2020: “Template for Retirement / 
Decommissioning Plan”.

If data is archived or stored in an existing system, it must be defined 
additionally which data processing capabilities still need to be in place. 
These need to be tested during the migration to target.

If data migration is planned, SOP 2006 “Migrate a GxP relevant 
computerized system” is followed.

If retirement and decommissioning are done as one step, then the 
requirements for retirement need to be included into the plan.

Migrate a GxP-relevant computerized system
RESTRICTED
1
Scope and Framework
1.1
Scope
This GSOP defines the processes and quality requirements of how to “Migrate a GxP-relevant 
computerized system”. 
It covers data migration necessary in case of system replacement, system update, system 
retirement/decommissioning, integration of external data (e.g. from another system) or change from 
one Software as a Service Provider to another. Data migrations occur at any stage of the system life 
cycle.
It applies cross-functionally to all systems including relevant IT infrastructure implemented, operated ,  
maintained or retired/decommissioned within the GxP regulated areas independent of whether they 
technically reside at Bayer, Bayer’s service providers or at a cloud provider (IaaS, PaaS or SaaS).
Not in scope of this procedure is software as an integral part of a medical device or classified itself as 
a medical device as well as computerized systems used within the PH GLP and GCIS areas.
1.2
Framework
This SOP describes a core process of the process group "Migrate a GxP-relevant computerized 
system". It addresses the phase of migrating a computerized system in the GxP regulated 
environment.
The overarching framework is provided in the general process document GSOP 2002 " Manage GxP 
computerized system life cycle ".
This process has interfaces with:
-
GSOP 2003: Set up and implement a computerized system in the GxP environment
-
GSOP 2004: Operate and maintain a GxP-relevant computerized system 
-
GSOP 2005: Retire and decommission a GxP-relevant computerized system 
2

3
Definitions
For generic definitions see GMP/GDP QMS Supplement 200 “Glossary”.
General definitions related to computerized systems in GxP environment, including definition of 
software categories 3-5 can be found in GSOP 2002.
Data migration 
Data migration is the activity of transporting electronic data from 
one system to another or simply the transition of data from one 
state to another. 
IQMS-35684 Ver. 4.0 | 2006
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2021-10-15
Page: 4 of 14 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-21 16:33 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

Migrate a GxP-relevant computerized system
RESTRICTED
4
Process Information
4.1
Purpose and general guidance
The aim of a data migration is that the data remains usable and retains its contextual meaning. Data 
authenticity and integrity must be maintained. In particular, it has to be ensured that any applicable 
data retained in the system will be accessible for the time specified by the most stringent applicable 
regulatory and / or legal requirements.
Migrations take place in the context of:
-
In-place version upgrade of a database or application.
-
Data conversion e.g. from one supplier's database to another.

metadata.titulo: Migrate a GxP-relevant computerized system
Migrate a GxP-relevant computerized system
Migrate a GxP-relevant computerized system

__data__.text:
Data 
authenticity and integrity must be maintained. In particular, it has to be ensured that any applicable 
data retained in the system will be accessible for the time specified by the most stringent applicable 
regulatory and / or legal requirements.
Migrations take place in the context of:
-
In-place version upgrade of a database or application.
-
Data conversion e.g. from one supplier's database to another.
-
Same system migration e.g. transporting from one server platform to another.
Migrations can take place from one source system to one target system or from multiple source 
systems to one target system. 
These vary largely in scope, complexity, and risk. Depending upon the type of migration, the steps to 
be performed vary. When migrating data, related electronic records, audit trails and electronic 
signatures, where applicable, are also included into migration.
Data migration is either managed as an integral part of an implementation project or a part of Change 
Management when new data is rolled into an already productive system. Change Management in 
accordance with GSOP 2004 applies for the source system.
Data migration is divided into the steps described in Section 5. In complex migrations, iterations 
between the steps are often necessary to achieve the expected data quality.
If migration is connected with the implementation of a new system in accordance with GSOP 2003 or 
functional changes, the testing of the system functionality has to be finalized and the results accepted 
as a prerequisite for finalizing planning and execution,. Stringent change control must be maintained 
over the system during data migration.
All documents and standard operating procedures referred in the framework and further in this 
document apply according to their specific scope.
It is possible to combine or split deliverables as necessary, as long as requirements concerning 
contents and approvals are met as given in the GSOPs.
Required documentation is created either by the role defined as accountable below or by an 
appropriate Subject Matter Expert for the deliverable concerned.
The procedures defined in this GSOP apply to all system categories 3, 4 and 5.

5.1.1
Create Data Migration Master Plan 

Describe scope, approach, schedule, and resources involved in the 
intended data migration activities, addressing the following items, or 
referencing the corresponding documentation referenced:

Migration requirements

Data migration approach


migration 



Deliverables

Consider during migration planning relevant master data / metadata. 
Checked and document whether master data / metadata is migrated 
automatically or whether it needs to be re-created manually following 
defined procedures.
Remarks:

A prerequisite for data migration is an approved change request 
documenting the decision to migrate.

It is recommended that migration planning starts as early in the project as 
feasible. Communication with all relevant parties (e.g. data owners, system 
users) is part of the migration planning.

During migration planning, relevant master data/metadata needs to be 
considered. 

Where migration is part of the implementation of a new system, the 
Validation Plan/Protocol is approved before the Migration Master Plan.

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference

Definition of data items to be migrated including relevant metadata and 
audit trail information

Data mapping between source and target systems

Business rules for data conversion.
5.1.4
Approve Data Migration Specification
Review and approve the Data Migration Specification.
PM (A), SO, QA
5.1.5
Create Data Migration Plan/Protocol 

Define how it is verified that the migrated data are complete, correct and 
usable as required. This includes:

Tools or methods to be used, including definition of used staging or 
intermediate storage areas

Tests to be performed for demonstrating correctness of the tools used, 
as applicable

Necessary data clean-up measures

Necessary data integrity checks before migration

Process for migrating the data to the environment where testing will be 
performed

Approach to User Acceptance Testing including, where used, statistical 
sampling in order to give evidence that data have not been altered after 
migration (actual data migration testing)

Acceptance criteria / accepted error levels

Deviation handling

Process for migrating the data to the productive environment if testing is 
done on a separate environment

Acceptable period for the system to be off-line

Duration of the conversion

Duration of the final data verification

The process for follow-up issues, e.g. Hypercare.
Remarks:

A template is provided in Best Practice 2022 “Template for Data Migration 
Plan/Protocol”

The level of testing the tools varies greatly depending on the complexity of 
the tools and of the data structure. Testing only the migrated data is 
acceptable. The testing approach and the required level for User 
Acceptance testing follow a risk-based approach based on the risks defined 
in the Migration Master Plan.

Where statistical sampling is used, a recommended sampling procedure is

Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference
5.2.1
Develop data migration tool

Develop load programs that prepare the data based on the approved 
conversion rules and then load the data into an extract file or database 
based on the approved mapping rules.
Remarks

The System Supplier(s) of the source and target systems are involved in 
the process as necessary. This is especially the case of migrations during 
upgrades or transfer between systems from different suppliers. 
Accountability remains with IT SP.
IT SP
5.2.2
Conduct data verification checks 

Migrate the data into a controlled test environment and perform verification 
that the migration tools support the requirements. Data Verification 
(integrity) checks are usually based on the conversion rules for the data 
cleanup. At this stage the finalized conversion rules will be enforced. 

If the business rules change based on the results of the data cleanup and 
normalization, update the Migration Specification.
IT SP
5.2.3
Clean up data

Clean up in parallel the source data and map the data from source to 
target system.

Use the defined business rules during cleanup of the source data. 
Remarks

This is an iterative process until the required level of correctness is 
achieved. This will depend on the data items and will not always be 100%.
PM 
5.2.4
Transfer data to test (Q) environment 

Transfer data into a controlled test environment.

Create a defined Staging area once the data cleanup is finalized to store 
extracts generated from the source data. 

Create separate Target Area to store the source data after it has been 
converted. Neither of these areas is part of the applications.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material

__data__.text:
7.0 | 209
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-11-01
Page: 3 of 14 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-23 17:36 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

INTERNAL
1
Scope and Framework
1.1
Scope
This GSOP defines the quality requirements for “Packaging and Labeling processes and packaging 
materials”.
It covers the implementation of the approved labeling texts.
It applies cross-functionally to all organizations, processes and products within the GMP/GDP and ISO 
regulated areas of Product Supply and related interfaces.
1.2
Framework 
This GSOP describes one core process of the process group “Supply Product” (GSOP 1747).
The overarching framework is provided in the General Process Document “Supply Product” (GSOP 
1747.).
This GSOP has interfaces with:
-
GOI 508 “Requirements for Serialized Barcodes” and its Supplement 1344 “Serialization Scope 
Books” for specific quality aspects and technical requirements.
-
GSOP 247 “Manage non-routine repackaging and relabeling of finished goods after distribution 
from their Original Packaging Site”
2
Abbreviations
NA
3
Definitions
For generic definitions see SUP 200 “Glossary”.
Combination Packs 
/ multi-item packs
Finished products containing more than one component that each have a 
separate batch number.
Labeling Material
All imprints or printed labels attached to primary or secondary packaging.
Tamper evident 
closure
A Packaging system that cannot be accessed without obvious destruction 
of the seal or some portion of the Packaging system. 
4
Purpose & Set-Up
4.1
Purpose of GSOP
The purpose of this document is to provide requirements for packaging and labeling processes.
4.2
General Guidance for the GSOP
4.2.1
Purchase of packaging materials
Packaging and labeling materials are purchased from qualified suppliers (see GOI 185 “Qualify GMP 
Material Supplier (API, excipient, critical disposable, packaging material)”.
4.2.2
Release of packaging materials
There are written procedures describing in sufficient detail the receipt, identification, quarantine, 
storage, handling, sampling, examination and/or testing and release of labeling and packaging 
materials. For all labeling and packaging materials an identification and revision system is in place for 
each different API, Drug Product, Medical Device, Cosmetic or Food Supplement.
IQMS-97208 Ver. 7.0 | 209
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-11-01
Page: 4 of 14 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-23 17:36 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

INTERNAL
4.2.3
Documentation and Record Retention
APIs, Drug Products, Medical Devices, Cosmetics or Food Supplements are identified with a batch or 
control number that permits the determination of the history of manufacture and control of the batch 
or Medical Device unit.
For combination packs/multi-item packs the secondary packaging material states only one batch 
number. For new launch products, the batch number on the secondary packaging material is a 
completely new and separate one. For existing products, it is also allowed to use the batch number of 
the leading component. For the latter, it has to be ensured that the expiry date of the leading 
component is the shortest one. The individual components of a multi-item pack might have different 
batch numbers.
The packaging process is documented. The labeling materials used for each production unit, lot or 
batch are documented.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material

__data__.text:
For new launch products, the batch number on the secondary packaging material is a 
completely new and separate one. For existing products, it is also allowed to use the batch number of 
the leading component. For the latter, it has to be ensured that the expiry date of the leading 
component is the shortest one. The individual components of a multi-item pack might have different 
batch numbers.
The packaging process is documented. The labeling materials used for each production unit, lot or 
batch are documented.
Specimens or copies of all labeling used in the batch are included in the batch record, where 
appropriate.
All records related to packaging and labeling processes including those related to serial numbers are 
kept for the specific retention period defined in SUP 1764 “Retention Periods for GMP/GDP relevant 
data (supplement to GSOP 257)”
4.2.4
Change Management for New or Changed Packaging and Labeling Materials
New or changed packaging material and labeling materials are introduced and controlled by a change 
management process which covers any suitability requirements, e.g., relevant for primary packaging 
material such as leachables. (see GSOP 503 “Manage change").
When in course of a change process (see previous paragraph) designing new packaging/shipping 
boxes or when changing packaging/shipping boxes it is ensured that the APIs, Drug Products, Medical 
Devices, Cosmetics or Food Supplements are protected against alteration, contamination or damage 
when exposed to expected conditions and hazards during processing, storage, handling, and 
distribution. 
Similar special requirements for radiopharmaceutical products’ packaging are considered in order to 
ensure suitability for their purpose and to ensure that the environment and personnel is appropriately 
protected when exposed to this type of products during processing, storage, handling and distribution.
Furthermore, for Medical Devices the significance is determined for those changes to function, 
performance, usability, safety and applicable regulatory requirements and its intended use. 
Prior to the implementation of marketed product in the production process, new or changed packaging 
materials and labeling materials for APIs, Drug Products, Medical Devices, Cosmetics or Food 
Supplements are approved by the regulatory affairs representative/relevant function. This decision is 
documented within the change management system.
4.3
Roles and responsibilities
Marketing 
Authorization 
Holder (MAH)
Ensures the correctness and completeness of the local label texts’ content
Ensures the compliance with the respective global documents (e.g. 
respective global GMP/GDP QMS documents such as GSOPs, GOIs as 
well as Global Reg. Affairs or global functional documents)
Ensures the compliance with any local guidelines
Ensures the generation, proof-reading and approval of such texts including 
applied codes and product identifiers like for instance the GTIN
Manufacturer
Ensures that each batch of packaging material used complies with the 
approved documents and are administrated and documented by a change 
management system (see GSOP 503 “Manage change").

INTERNAL
5
Detailed description (Requirements and Procedures)
5.1
Environment and Machine Set-up
Description
Assigned Roles & 
Document Reference

Perform packaging and labeling operations in the appropriate cleanliness 
classes (see GSOP 268 “Quality requirements for Clean Rooms”).

Outline packaging equipment parameters and settings in written procedures 
where appropriate and display name and batch number of the product being 
handled at each packaging station or line.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material

__data__.text:

Outline packaging equipment parameters and settings in written procedures 
where appropriate and display name and batch number of the product being 
handled at each packaging station or line.
Remarks:

For implantable medical devices conditions for the work environment during 
packaging operations comply with the specified safety and performance 
requirements. Work conditions used for packaging and labeling of 
implantable medical devices are recorded
GSOP 268
5.2
Storage and transport of packaging materials
Description
Document Reference

Store packaging and labeling materials for each different API, Drug 
Product, Medical Device, Cosmetic or Food Supplement, strength, dosage 
form or quantity of contents in a manner that provides proper identification 
and is designed to prevent mix-ups.

Limit access to labeling material and control storage areas access to 
authorized personnel at all times, also on the shop floor. 

Store and transport cut labels and other loose printed materials in separate 
and sealed/locked containers to avoid mix-ups and unauthorized access at 
all times.

Clearly mark nonconforming material and product as well as unused batch 
coded material and store separately, either in restricted areas or in a 
manner which hinders unauthorized access. 

Prevent mix-ups, substitution and cross-contamination by physical or 
spatial separation from other operations.

Seal API containers, that are to be transported outside of the 
manufacturer’s control, in a manner to allow for identification of breached 
or missing seals.
IQMS-97208 Ver. 7.0 | 209
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2024-11-01
Page: 6 of 14 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-23 17:36 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

INTERNAL
5.3
Packaging Operations
Description
Document Reference
5.3.1
Line Clearance

Inspect the packaging and labeling facilities immediately before use for 
line-clearance to assure that all materials, products and documents from 
previous operations and not needed for the next packaging operation have 
been removed according to an approved local procedure. 

Document the results of the inspection in the batch production records or 
device history records.
5.3.2
Packaging Process

Assure that the correct labeling materials (including batch number, expiry 
date, manufacturing date or storage conditions, where appropriate) and 
packaging materials are used including checking quantity, identity and 
conformity with the packaging instructions upon delivery to the packaging 
department.

In case unlabeled primary containers are subject to packaging operations 
identify them (e.g., but not limited to by analytical methods, specific 
embossing or specific color codes of crimping caps and lids) prior use to 
avoid mix-ups and mislabeling.

Ensure that containers for filling are clean before filling either by using 
ready to use containers cleaned beforehand and the cleanliness 
maintained until use or by suitable washing before use.

Avoid and remove any contaminants such as glass fragments and metal 
particles.

Examine the packaging and labeling materials for suitability (e.g. related to 
damages, dirt or other issues hindering the use of the material) and 
correctness before packaging operations commence and document it in a 
batch production record.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material

__data__.text:

Ensure that containers for filling are clean before filling either by using 
ready to use containers cleaned beforehand and the cleanliness 
maintained until use or by suitable washing before use.

Avoid and remove any contaminants such as glass fragments and metal 
particles.

Examine the packaging and labeling materials for suitability (e.g. related to 
damages, dirt or other issues hindering the use of the material) and 
correctness before packaging operations commence and document it in a 
batch production record.

For Aspirin 0.1g and 0.3 g enteric coated tablets: Perform the 
packaging line adjustment test according to Annex 1 Packaging Line 
Adjustment Test for Aspirin 0.1 g and 0.3 g Enteric Coated – 
Resistance to Water

Do not re-introduce samples taken away from the packaging line into the 
packaging process to avoid mix-ups.

Only re-introduce product involved in an unusual event such as rejects 
either into the same packaging process step or at the previous packaging 
process step.

Remove and destroy packaging material used in the process step during 
which the unusual event occurred.

Only reintroduce product in the same packaging process step with detailed 
records and after a special inspection, investigation and approval by 
authorized personnel following the Four-Eyes- Principle.

In case product is reintroduced in the previous packaging step define 
measures based on a risk analysis considering the installed equipment, 
existing dedicated possibilities to reintroduce material as well as housing 
and coverage of the packaging line hindering any reintroduction of material 
at other than the dedicated places. These measures include detailed 
records and minimum one of the following measures: inspections,

Description
Document Reference
investigations, approval by authorized personnel or application of the Four-
Eyes-Principle.

Regularly check printing devices on, or associated with, manufacturing 
lines used to imprint labeling upon the Drug Product, Medical Device, 
Cosmetic or Food Supplement unit label or case are regularly checked e.g. 
prior to starting the packaging process to assure that all imprinting 
conforms to the print specified in the batch production record or device 
master record and document the results.

Regularly check any printing devices, cameras or electronic 
readers/scanners used in course of (serialized) code application and 
control e.g. prior to starting the packaging process to assure proper 
functioning and document the results.
Remarks:

Once product is involved in unusual events special attention is given to the 
status of the serialized code in case product is re-introduced. The code 
actually represents the status of the affected pack and is adapted 
accordingly. The rules as defined in GOI 508 “Requirements for Serialized 
Barcodes” apply. 
GOI 508
5.3.3
In-process controls

Check and record the correct performance of any printing or embossing 
operation and ensure that the printed and/or embossed information is 
readable, distinct and resistant to fading or erasing.

Check at least for initial control for release of the packaging line or process 
and on-line control during packaging the following:

General appearance of the packages (e. g. correctness of closure or 
sealing of primary containers (tamper evident closure), legibility of 
printed data, correct label information such as expiry dates, 
manufacturing. dates or batch numbers), application of a (serialized)

b)
Whether the packages are complete
c)
Whether the correct products and packaging materials are used
d)
Correct functioning of line monitors

Document the results of the In-Process-Controls
5.3.4
Reconciliation

Perform a reconciliation of the amount of bulk product, labeling and 
packaging materials at the completion of packaging.

Investigate any significant or unusual discrepancy observed during 
reconciliation of the amount of bulk product and printed packaging 
materials and the number of units produced and account satisfactorily 
before release.

Upon completion of packaging operations clearly mark, store separately 
and finally destroy unused batch-coded material, rejects and any other 
nonconforming material and document in the batch record.

Refer to GOI 508 “Requirements for Serialized Barcodes” and its 
Supplement 1344 “Serialization Scope Books” for specific reconciliation 
rules once serialized codes are applied to drug product packaging.

9
Annex 1 Packaging Line Adjustment Test for Aspirin 0.1 g and 0.3 g Enteric 
Coated – Resistance to Water
Aspirin cardio tablets are sensitive to mechanical stress that might occur during packaging operations.
The packaging line adjustment test is performed to recognize damaged tablets which are not resistant to 
water during test time.
The test is performed after adjustment of packaging machine before production starts and in case there is 
any readjustment of product contacting parts of the packaging machine.
Description of the Test
1.
Carefully remove 300 tablets out of blisters and transfer them into a suitable container (e.g. stainless 
steel 30 cm x 20 cm x 5 cm). 
2.
Add 1 liter of tap water. 
3.
Ensure that all tablets are covered by water and that the tablets are placed as one layer.
4.
Examine conditions for 5 min.
Evaluation of the Test

The test has been successfully completed if no tablet out of the 300 tablets (level I) disintegrated.

metadata.titulo: Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material
Requirements for Packaging, Labeling and for Packaging Material

__data__.text:
Description of the Test
1.
Carefully remove 300 tablets out of blisters and transfer them into a suitable container (e.g. stainless 
steel 30 cm x 20 cm x 5 cm). 
2.
Add 1 liter of tap water. 
3.
Ensure that all tablets are covered by water and that the tablets are placed as one layer.
4.
Examine conditions for 5 min.
Evaluation of the Test

The test has been successfully completed if no tablet out of the 300 tablets (level I) disintegrated.

If there are one or two tablets disintegrated out of 300 a second test with additional 300 tablets is 
performed (level II). A maximum of 2 disintegrated tablets are allowed in 600 tested tablets to pass 
the level II requirement.

If more than 2 out of 300/600 tablets are found, the test fails. In such a situation the packaging line is 
re-adjusted and the test has to be repeated.

If the test fails during production a deviation report is issued and furthermore the quality of the batch 
evaluated according to AM ALQ 92 first additional test (200 tablets).

1 
Scope 
This SOP defines “Quality Requirements for Cleanrooms and Associated Controlled Environments”. 
It covers: 
- 
Requirements and specifications for design, layout, maintenance and operation of cleanroom 
premises and associated controlled environments 
- 
Requirements for personnel and materials 
- 
Principles for minimizing the risk of errors, permitting effective cleaning and maintenance, avoiding 
contamination and cross-contamination, and preventing any adverse effect on product quality 
It applies cross-functionally to: 
- 
All manufacturing and development sites of divisions in scope  
- 
APIs, IMPs, Drug Products (incl. combination products, Biologics and ATMPs). All these are in 
this GSOP collectively referred to as the ‘product’.

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
combination products, Biologics and ATMPs). All these are in 
this GSOP collectively referred to as the ‘product’. 
 
Not in Scope are: 
- 
Medical Device production sites (refer to GSOP 1138 “Requirements for Medical Devices - Site, 
Facilities, and Equipment Control”) 
 
2 
Abbreviations 
APS 
Aseptic Process Simulation 
ATMP 
Advanced Therapy Medicinal Product 
CCS 
Contamination Control Strategy 
CFU 
Colony Forming Unit 
CNC 
Controlled-non-classified 
HEPA 
High efficiency particulate air filter 
HVAC 
Heating, ventilation, and air conditioning 
IMP 
Investigational Medicinal Product 
MAL 
Material Airlock 
PAL 
Personnel Airlock 
RABS 
Restricted Access Barrier System 
 
3 
Definitions 
For generic definitions see SUP 200 “Glossary”. 
Airlock 
An enclosed space with two or more doors, constructed to maintain air 
pressure control between adjoining rooms (generally with different air 
cleanliness standards). The intent of an airlock is to preclude ingress of 
particle matter and microorganism contamination from a lesser controlled 
area. Airlocks are transition spaces rather than classified rooms 
themselves. 
An airlock is designed for and used by people and/or goods (personnel 
airlock (PAL); material airlock (MAL). 
IQMS-46277 Ver. 9.0 | 268
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2026-01-01
Page: 4 of 28 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-23 17:30 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

 
 
Change room 
Change rooms are typically designed as airlocks and used to provide 
physical separation of the different stages of changing and to minimize 
microbial and particulate contamination of protective clothing. 
Cleanroom 
A room designed, maintained, and controlled to prevent particle and 
microbial contamination of products. Such a room is assigned and 
reproducibly meets an appropriate air cleanliness level. 
Cleanroom Classification 
A method of assessing the level of air cleanliness against a specification 
for a cleanroom or clean air equipment by measuring the total particle 
concentration. 
Closed system 
A system in which the product is not exposed to the surrounding 
environment. For example, this can be achieved by the use of bulk product 
holders (such as tanks or bags) that are connected to each other by pipes 
or tubes as a system, and where used for sterile products, the full system 
is sterilized after the connections are made. 
Containment 
A process or device to contain product, dust or contaminants in one zone, 
preventing it from escaping to another zone 
Controlled-non-classified 
(CNC) Area 
Area for which no specifications for microbial contamination or total 
airborne particles are applied. Requirements to ensure control of the area 
are defined as e.g. material of construction, access control or pest control, 
i.e. classes F1 and F2. 
Critical zone/ area 
(sterile manufacturing) 
A location within the aseptic processing area in which product and critical 
surfaces are exposed to the environment. 
First Air 
(sterile manufacturing) 
Filtered air that has not been interrupted prior to contacting exposed 
product and product contact surfaces with the potential to add 
contamination to the air prior to reaching the critical zone. 
Isolator  
(sterile manufacturing) 
An enclosure capable of being subject to reproducible interior bio-
decontamination, with an internal work zone meeting grade A conditions 
that provides uncompromised, continuous isolation of its interior from the 
external environment (e.g. surrounding cleanroom air and personnel).

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
First Air 
(sterile manufacturing) 
Filtered air that has not been interrupted prior to contacting exposed 
product and product contact surfaces with the potential to add 
contamination to the air prior to reaching the critical zone. 
Isolator  
(sterile manufacturing) 
An enclosure capable of being subject to reproducible interior bio-
decontamination, with an internal work zone meeting grade A conditions 
that provides uncompromised, continuous isolation of its interior from the 
external environment (e.g. surrounding cleanroom air and personnel). 
There are two major types of isolators: 
• Closed isolator systems exclude external contamination of the 
isolator’s interior by accomplishing material transfer via aseptic 
connection to auxiliary equipment, rather than use of openings to the 
surrounding environment. Closed systems remain sealed throughout 
operations. 
• Open isolator systems are designed to allow for the continuous or 
semi-continuous ingress and/or egress of materials during operations 
through one or more openings. Openings are engineered (e.g. using 
continuous overpressure) to exclude the entry of external 
contaminant into the isolator. 
Pass-Through Box 
A cabinet with two or more doors for passing equipment, material or 
product while maintaining the pressure cascade and segregation between 
two controlled zones. A passive pass-through box has no air supply or air 
extraction. A dynamic pass-through box has an air supply into the chamber. 
Synonyms: pass-through hatch, pass box 
Restricted Access Barrier 
System (RABS) 
System that provides an enclosed, but not fully sealed, environment 
meeting defined air quality conditions (for aseptic processing grade A), and 
using a rigid-wall enclosure and integrated gloves to separate its interior 
from the surrounding cleanroom environment. The inner surfaces of the 
RABS are disinfected and decontaminated with a sporicidal agent. 
IQMS-46277 Ver. 9.0 | 268
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2026-01-01
Page: 5 of 28 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-23 17:30 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

 
 
Operators use gloves, half suits, rapid transfer ports (RTPs) and other 
integrated transfer ports to perform manipulations or convey materials to 
the interior of the RABS. Depending on the design, doors are rarely 
opened, and only under strictly pre-defined conditions. 
Sterile Product 
Refers to one or more of the sterilized elements exposed to aseptic 
conditions and ultimately making up the sterile active substance or finished 
sterile product. These elements include the containers, closures, and 
components of the finished drug product. Or, a product that is rendered 
sterile by a terminal sterilization process. 
Unidirectional Air flow 
An airflow moving in a single direction, in a robust and uniform manner, and 
at sufficient velocity, to reproducibly sweep particles away from the critical 
processing or testing area. 
 
 
IQMS-46277 Ver. 9.0 | 268
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2026-01-01
Page: 6 of 28 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-23 17:30 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

 
 
4 
Purpose  
4.1 
Purpose of this document 
This document defines requirements for cleanrooms and the associated controlled environments in 
order to minimize the risks of chemical, particulate, and microbial (as well as endotoxin/pyrogen for 
sterile products) contamination.

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
It is complemented by the BP 1910 “Best Practice to GSOP 268 “Quality requirements for cleanrooms 
and associated controlled environments”, which provides additional guidance on the implementation 
of the requirements. 
Products must be protected from contamination by other products (cross-contamination), by 
microorganisms, and by any other foreign matter. 
Possible contamination sources from the environment are: 
- 
premises and equipment 
- 
air 
- 
personnel 
- 
material. 
 
Protection from contamination is achieved by ensuring appropriate design, construction, adaptation, 
maintenance, and operation of premises and equipment, and is supported by the application of Quality 
Risk Management principles to assess and control all risks. 
To minimize the contamination risk to acceptable levels, manufacturing areas at BAYER are assigned 
to seven cleanliness classes (A, B, C, D, E, F1 and F2) (see chapter 5.2). 
Additional local (country specific) GMP requirements for countries outside of Europe and United States 
may be required for the manufacture of product designated for these countries. (e.g. GOI 576 “Country 
Specific GMP/GDP Requirements for Mexico”, GOI 647 “Country Specific GMP/GDP Requirements 
for the P.R. of China”). 
Additionally, for hazardous materials (OEB4 and higher) both, HSE and GMP aspects have to be 
considered to reach a holistic technical and organizational cleanroom concept. 
 
Cleanrooms undergo (re-)qualification and classified environments also require monitoring: 
- Refer to GSOP 1447 “Requirements for Qualification of Clean Rooms” 
- Refer to GSOP 1578 “Manage Monitoring” 
 
 
IQMS-46277 Ver. 9.0 | 268
Subtype: Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) | Effective Date: 2026-01-01
Page: 7 of 28 | Internal
Export Date: 2026-06-23 17:30 GMT+01:00 (Copy is not tracked by the system)

 
 
5 
Detailed description 
5.1 
Quality Risk Management (QRM) 
Description including document reference 
- 
Determine the extent of technical and organizational measures required to control risks for 
contamination and cross-contamination based on a quality risk management process.  
- 
Manage processes, equipment, facilities and manufacturing activities in accordance with QRM 
principles to provide a proactive means of identifying, scientifically evaluating and controlling 
potential risks to quality. In the first instance, QRM priorities include appropriate design of the 
facility, equipment and processes, followed by the implementation of well-designed procedures, 
and finally application of monitoring systems as the element that demonstrates that the design and 
procedures have been correctly implemented and continue to perform in line with expectations. 
- 
Use the Quality Risk Management process, which includes a potency and toxicological evaluation, 
to assess and control the cross-contamination risks presented by the products manufactured. 
Factors including facility/equipment design and use, personnel and material flow, microbiological 
controls, physico-chemical characteristics of the active substance, process characteristics, cleaning 
processes and analytical capabilities relative to the relevant limits established from the evaluation 
of the products are also be taken into account. The outcome of the Quality Risk Management 
process is the basis for determining the necessity for and extent to which premises and equipment 
are dedicated to a particular product or product family. This may include dedicating specific product 
contact parts or dedication of the entire manufacturing facility. It may be acceptable to confine 
manufacturing activities to a segregated, self-contained production area within a multiproduct 
facility, where justified.

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
The outcome of the Quality Risk Management 
process is the basis for determining the necessity for and extent to which premises and equipment 
are dedicated to a particular product or product family. This may include dedicating specific product 
contact parts or dedication of the entire manufacturing facility. It may be acceptable to confine 
manufacturing activities to a segregated, self-contained production area within a multiproduct 
facility, where justified. 
- 
Refer to GSOP 1040 “Requirements for Prevention of Cross-Contamination in Shared Facilities” 
- 
Refer to GSOP 105 “Manage Quality Risk” 
- 
Refer to GSOP 282 “Approaches to Viral Safety in Facilities, Processes and Equipment” 
 
In addition for Sterile Manufacturing: 
- 
Implement a Contamination Control Strategy (CCS) across the facility in order to define all critical 
control points and assess the effectiveness of all the controls (design, procedural, technical and 
organizational) and monitoring measures employed to manage risks to medicinal product quality 
and safety.  
- 
Refer to GOI 1700 “Manage contamination control strategy” 
 
 
5.2 
Cleanliness Classes 
Description including document reference 
5.2.1 
General Requirements for Cleanliness Classes 
- 
Premises are situated in an environment which, when considered together with measures to protect 
the manufacture, presents minimal risk of causing contamination of materials or products. 
The manufacture of products is carried out in appropriate cleanrooms. Cleanrooms and airlocks are 
maintained to an appropriate cleanliness standard and supplied with air that has passed through filters 
of an appropriate efficiency. Controls and monitoring are scientifically justified and effectively evaluate 
the state of environmental conditions of cleanrooms, airlocks and pass-through boxes. 
 
5.2.2 
Assignment of Cleanliness Classes 
- 
Assign cleanliness classes (A - F2) depending on the activities carried out.  
Classes A-E represent ‘classified’ areas in which limits for microbial contamination and total

Description including document reference 
airborne particles are specified.  
Classes F1/F2 are ‘controlled-non-classified’ areas. No limits for microbial contamination and total 
airborne particles are required. 
▪ Class F2: controlled-non-classified (CNC) area with normal housekeeping and maintenance, 
where there is no potential for product contamination, e.g. warehousing 
▪ Class F1: controlled-non-classified (CNC) area in which steps are taken to protect the 
pharmaceutical starting material or product from direct or indirect contamination or degradation, 
e.g. secondary packing 
▪ Class E: Classified area in which specific environmental conditions are defined, controlled and 
monitored, to prevent contamination or degradation of the product. Appropriate for 
manufacturing and primary packaging of non-sterile products that are usually at low risk for 
microbial contamination, e.g. where product, starting materials and components are exposed to 
the room environment; plus equipment wash and storage areas for non-sterile equipment 
product contact parts. 
▪ Class C/D: Classified areas used for carrying out less critical stages in the manufacture of 
aseptically filled sterile products or as a background for isolators. They can also be used for the 
preparation/filling of terminally sterilized products. 
▪ Class B: For aseptic preparation and filling, this is the background cleanroom for grade A 
(where it is not an isolator). Air pressure differences are continuously monitored.

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
▪ Class C/D: Classified areas used for carrying out less critical stages in the manufacture of 
aseptically filled sterile products or as a background for isolators. They can also be used for the 
preparation/filling of terminally sterilized products. 
▪ Class B: For aseptic preparation and filling, this is the background cleanroom for grade A 
(where it is not an isolator). Air pressure differences are continuously monitored. Cleanrooms of 
lower grade than grade B can be considered where isolator technology is used. 
▪ Class A: The critical zone for high-risk operations (e.g. aseptic processing line, filling zone, 
stopper bowl, open primary packaging or for making aseptic connections under the protection 
of first air). Normally, such conditions are provided by a localized airflow protection, such as 
unidirectional airflow workstations within RABS or isolators. The maintenance of unidirectional 
airflow is demonstrated and qualified across the whole of the grade A area. Direct intervention 
(e.g. without the protection of barrier and glove port technology) into the grade A area by 
operators is minimized by premises, equipment, process and procedural design. 
- 
Refer to Annex 1 “Maximum permitted total airborne particle concentration for monitoring/ 
Maximum action limits for viable particle contamination” 
- 
For Sterile Manufacturing refer to Annex 2 “Examples of operations and grades for Sterile 
Manufacturing” 
- 
QC Laboratories: not assigned to a cleanliness class 
▪ 
Apply standards of the respective cleanliness class in case the laboratory is sited in a 
classified area (e.g. IPC laboratory).  
▪ 
Refer to GSOP 1490 “Requirements for QC testing” 
 
Remarks: 
- 
Cleanliness class-terminology for classes A to D as used throughout this GSOP is derived from the 
European GMP-Guide Volume 4, Annex 1 “Manufacture of Sterile Medicinal Products” (6). 
- 
Corresponding grades established in US FDA- regulations and ISO standards are compared in the 
associated Best Practice 1910 “BP to GSOP 268 ‘Requirements for Cleanliness Classes’”. 
- 
The cleanliness class-terminology for classes E, F1 and F2 as used throughout this GSOP is 
derived from WHO Technical Report Series, No. 1019, Annex 2 “Guidelines on heating, ventilation 
and air-conditioning systems for non-sterile pharmaceutical products” (13). Class F2 corresponds to 
“Level 1”, class F1 corresponds to “Level 2”, and class E corresponds to “Level 3”.

5.3 
Design and Layout of Premises and Equipment 
Description including document reference 
5.3.1 
Premises 
− 
Buildings and facilities are located, designed, and constructed to facilitate cleaning, maintenance, 
and operations as appropriate to the type and stage of manufacture. Facilities are also designed to 
minimize potential contamination. Where microbiological specifications have been established, 
facilities are designed to limit exposure to objectionable microbiological contaminants as 
appropriate. 
− 
Premises are preferably laid out in such a way as to allow the production to take place in areas 
connected in a logical order corresponding to the sequence of the operations and to the requisite 
cleanliness levels.

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
Facilities are also designed to 
minimize potential contamination. Where microbiological specifications have been established, 
facilities are designed to limit exposure to objectionable microbiological contaminants as 
appropriate. 
− 
Premises are preferably laid out in such a way as to allow the production to take place in areas 
connected in a logical order corresponding to the sequence of the operations and to the requisite 
cleanliness levels. 
− 
The adequacy of the working and in-process storage space permits the orderly and logical 
positioning of equipment and materials so as to minimize the risk of mixup between different 
products or their components, to avoid cross-contamination and to minimize the risk of omission or 
wrong application of any of the manufacturing or control steps. 
− 
The entry of unauthorized people is prevented. Production, storage and quality control areas are 
not used as a right of way by personnel who do not work in them. 
− 
Premises are designed and equipped so as to afford maximum protection against the entry of 
insects or other animals. 
− 
Where starting and primary packaging materials, intermediate or bulk products are exposed to the 
environment, interior surfaces (walls, floors and ceilings) are smooth, free from cracks and open 
joints, and do not shed particulate matter and permit easy and effective cleaning and, if necessary, 
disinfection. 
− 
Pipework, light fittings, ventilation points and other services are designed and sited to avoid the 
creation of recesses which are difficult to clean. As far as possible, for maintenance purposes, they 
are accessible from outside the manufacturing areas. 
− 
Drains are of adequate size and have trapped gullies. Open channels are avoided where possible, 
but if necessary, they are shallow to facilitate cleaning and disinfection. 
− 
In cases where dust is generated (e.g. during sampling, weighing, mixing and processing 
operations, packaging of dry products), specific provisions are taken to avoid cross-contamination 
and facilitate cleaning. 
− 
Weighing of starting materials usually is carried out in a separate weighing room designed for such 
use. 
− 
Where open product is handled: Ceilings are designed and sealed to prevent contamination from 
the space above them. 
− 
Adequate lighting is provided in all areas to facilitate cleaning, maintenance, and proper operations, 
particularly where visual on-line controls are carried out. 
− 
For API manufacturing: Where the equipment itself (e.g., closed or contained systems) provides 
adequate protection of the product, such equipment can be located outdoors. 
 
In addition for Sterile Manufacturing: 
− 
Facilities are designed to permit observation of production activities from outside the grade A and B 
areas (e.g. through the provision of windows or remote cameras with a full view of the area and 
processes to allow observation and supervision without entry). This requirement is considered 
when designing new facilities or during refurbishment of existing facilities. 
− 
To reduce accumulation of dust and to facilitate cleaning there are no recesses that are difficult to 
clean effectively, therefore projecting ledges, shelves, cupboards and equipment are kept to a 
minimum. Doors are designed to avoid recesses that cannot be cleaned. Sliding doors are 
undesirable for this reason. Examples of adequate design features include seamless and rounded 
floor to wall junctions as well as readily accessible corners. 
− 
Materials used in cleanrooms, both in the construction of the room and for items used within the 
room, are selected to minimize generation of particles and to permit the repeated application of 
cleaning, disinfectant and sporicidal agents where used.

Description including document reference 
− 
Sinks and drains are prohibited in the grade A and grade B areas. In other cleanrooms, air breaks 
are fitted between the machine or sink and the drains. Floor drains in lower grade cleanrooms are 
fitted with traps or water seals designed to prevent back flow and are regularly cleaned, disinfected 
and maintained. 
− 
Ceilings are designed and sealed to prevent contamination from the space above them. 
− 
The layout of equipment provides for ergonomics that optimize comfort and movement of operators. 
The number of personnel in an aseptic processing room is minimized. The flow of personnel is 
designed to limit the frequency with which entries and exits are made to and from an aseptic 
processing room and, most significant, its critical area. Regarding the latter, the number of transfers 
into the critical area of a traditional cleanroom, or an isolator, is minimized. To prevent changes in 
air currents that introduce lower quality air, movement adjacent to the critical area is appropriately 
restricted. 
 
5.3.1.1 
Toilets / Personnel Washing Facilities / Rest Rooms 
− 
Adequate, clean washing and toilet facilities are provided for personnel. These washing facilities 
are equipped with hot and cold water as appropriate, soap or detergent, air driers or single service 
towels. Adequate facilities for showering and/or changing clothes are provided, when appropriate. 
− 
Facilities for changing clothes and for washing and toilet purposes are easily accessible and 
appropriate for the number of users. Toilets do not directly communicate with production or storage 
areas. 
− 
Rest and refreshment rooms are separate from production areas. 
 
5.3.1.2 
Airlocks 
− 
To minimize the transfer of contamination between rooms of different cleanroom classes during 
personnel and material transfer, a physical segregation by means of an airlock and/or pass-through 
box is provided. The airlock and/or pass-through box maintains the pressure differential established 
between the rooms by ensuring the opposing doors are not opened simultaneously.  
− 
Airlocks are designed and used to provide physical separation and to minimize microbial and 
particle contamination of the different areas/zones and are present for material and personnel 
moving between different grades. Wherever possible, airlocks used for personnel movement are 
separated from those used for material movement. Where this is not practical, time-based 
separation of movement (personnel/ material) by procedure is utilized. Airlocks are flushed 
effectively with filtered air to ensure that the grade of the cleanroom is maintained. The final stage 
of the airlock is, in the “at rest” state, of the same cleanliness grade (viable and total particle) as the 
cleanroom into which it leads.

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
Wherever possible, airlocks used for personnel movement are 
separated from those used for material movement. Where this is not practical, time-based 
separation of movement (personnel/ material) by procedure is utilized. Airlocks are flushed 
effectively with filtered air to ensure that the grade of the cleanroom is maintained. The final stage 
of the airlock is, in the “at rest” state, of the same cleanliness grade (viable and total particle) as the 
cleanroom into which it leads. 
− 
In change rooms where de-gowning and gowning is conducted: To provide the required protection, 
consideration is given to different functional zones: 
a) areas configured for removal, storage, disposal and/or redonning of garments not permitted 
within the cleanroom; 
b) area where garments or personal equipment dedicated to the cleanroom are stored, donned 
or removed is established; 
c) area, in which gown integrity is also checked upon entry into the production area is 
established. Use of a full-length mirror is established to ensure that all items of clothing are 
properly adjusted. 
− 
Stepover benches or other clear demarcation systems are included within an airlock system for the 
passage of personnel. 
− 
For airlocks leading to grade C and D areas, a visual and/or audible warning system is operated as 
a minimum to avoid the doors being opened simultaneously. Where required to maintain area 
segregation, a time delay between the closing and opening of interlocked doors is established.

Description including document reference 
In addition for Sterile Manufacturing: 
− 
The use of separate change rooms for entering and leaving the grade B area is preferred. Where 
this is not practical, time-based separation of activities (ingress/egress) by procedure is 
established. Where the CCS indicates that the risk of contamination is high, separate change 
rooms for entering and leaving production areas are used. 
− 
For pass-through boxes and airlocks (for material and personnel), the entry and exit doors are not 
opened simultaneously. For airlocks leading to the grade A and grade B areas, an interlocking 
system is used. 
− 
Areas of increasing cleanliness are used for entry of personnel (e.g. from the grade D area to the 
grade C area to the grade B area). In general, hand washing facilities are provided only in the first 
stage of the change room and are not present in change rooms directly accessing the grade B 
area. 
− 
The transfer of equipment and materials into and out of the cleanrooms and critical zones is one of 
the greatest potential sources of contamination. Any activities with the potential to compromise the 
cleanliness of cleanrooms or the critical zone are assessed and if they cannot be eliminated, 
appropriate controls are implemented. 
− 
Pass-through boxes are designed to protect the higher-grade environment, for example by effective 
flushing with an active filtered air supply. 
− 
The movement of material or equipment from lower grade or unclassified area to higher-grade 
clean areas is subject to cleaning and disinfection commensurate with the risk and in line with the 
CCS.

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
Any activities with the potential to compromise the 
cleanliness of cleanrooms or the critical zone are assessed and if they cannot be eliminated, 
appropriate controls are implemented. 
− 
Pass-through boxes are designed to protect the higher-grade environment, for example by effective 
flushing with an active filtered air supply. 
− 
The movement of material or equipment from lower grade or unclassified area to higher-grade 
clean areas is subject to cleaning and disinfection commensurate with the risk and in line with the 
CCS. 
 
5.3.2 
Equipment 
− 
Equipment is constructed so that surfaces that contact components, in-process materials, or 
products are not reactive, additive, or absorptive so as to alter the safety, identity, strength, quality, 
or purity of the product beyond official or other established requirements. 
− 
Washing and cleaning equipment is chosen and used in order not to be a source of contamination. 
− 
If necessary: Equipment is appropriately designed to facilitate ease of sterilization. The effect of 
equipment design on the cleanroom environment is addressed. Horizontal surfaces or ledges that 
accumulate particles are avoided. Equipment does not obstruct airflow, and, in critical areas, its 
design does not disturb unidirectional airflow. 
 
In addition for Sterile Manufacturing: 
− 
It is important to ensure ease of installation to facilitate aseptic setup. 
− 
As far as practicable, equipment, fittings and services are designed and installed so that 
operations, maintenance, and repairs can be performed outside the cleanroom. If maintenance has 
to be performed in the cleanroom, and the required standards of cleanliness and/or asepsis cannot 
be maintained, then precautions such as restricting access to the work area to specified personnel, 
generation of clearly defined work protocols and maintenance procedures are established. 
Additional cleaning, disinfection and environmental monitoring are defined for any such 
maintenance issues. If sterilization of equipment is required, it is carried out, wherever possible, 
after complete reassembly. 
− 
The design of equipment used in aseptic processing limits the number and complexity of aseptic 
interventions by personnel. 
− 
For aseptic processes, direct and indirect product contact parts are sterilized. Direct product 
contact parts are those that the product passes through, such as filling needles or pumps. Indirect 
product contact parts are equipment parts that do not contact the product, but may come into 
contact with other sterilized surfaces, the sterility of which is critical to the overall product sterility 
(e.g. sterilized items such as stopper bowls and guides, and sterilized components). 
− 
Conveyor belts do not pass through a partition between a grade A or B area and a processing area 
of lower air cleanliness, unless the belt itself is continually sterilized (e.g. in a sterilizing tunnel).

5.4 
Ventilation 
Description including document reference 
5.4.1 
General Requirements for Ventilation 
- 
Temperature, humidity and ventilation are appropriate and do not adversely affect, directly or 
indirectly, either the products during their manufacture and storage, or the accurate functioning of 
equipment. 
- 
Adequate ventilation, air filtration and exhaust systems are provided, where required and/or 
identified via QRM principles.

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
- 
Adequate ventilation, air filtration and exhaust systems are provided, where required and/or 
identified via QRM principles. These systems are designed and constructed to minimize risks of 
contamination and cross-contamination and include equipment for control of air pressure, 
microorganisms (if appropriate), dust, humidity, and temperature, as appropriate to the stage of 
manufacture. Particular attention is given to areas where product is exposed to the environment. 
- 
Refer to country specific requirements in case products for the associated markets are produced 
(e.g. GOI 576 “Country Specific GMP/GDP Requirements for Mexico”, GOI 647 “Country Specific 
GMP/GDP Requirements for the P.R. of China”) 
▪ Secondary packaging for Mexico: Positive pressure to non-classified areas is required 
 
5.4.2 
Air Filtration 
- 
Air filtration systems, including prefilters and particulate matter air filters, are used when required 
and/or identified via QRM principles on air supplies to production areas. If air is recirculated to 
production areas, measures are taken to control recirculation of dust from production. In areas 
where air contamination may occur during production, there are adequate exhaust systems or other 
systems adequate to control contaminants.  
- 
HEPA filter integrity must be maintained, where the use of HEPA filters has been determined. Leak 
testing is performed at installation to detect integrity breaches around the sealing gaskets, through 
the frames, or through various points on the filter media. Thereafter, leak tests are performed at 
suitable time intervals for terminal HEPA filters. 
- 
Refer to GSOP 1447 “Requirements for Qualification of Clean Rooms” and BP 1626 “Qualification 
of HVAC-Systems” 
 
In addition for Sterile Manufacturing: 
- 
The integrity of critical air vent filters (that are directly linked to the sterility of the product) are 
verified by testing after use, with the filter remaining in the filter assembly or housing. 
 
5.4.3 
Pressure Differentials/ Air Flow/ Air Change Rates 
- 
Cleanrooms are supplied with filtered air that maintains a pressure difference and/or an airflow 
relative to the environment of a lower grade under all operational conditions and flushes the area 
effectively. 
- 
In case containment of certain materials is necessary (e.g., pathogenic, highly toxic or radioactive 
products or live viral or bacterial materials): air supply and pressure differentials can be modified 
(e.g., positively or negatively pressurized airlocks) that prevent the hazardous material from 
contaminating surrounding areas. It is assessed and considered if decontamination of facilities and 
the treatment of air leaving the clean area is necessary. 
- 
For examples on bubble or sinks refer to BP 1910 
 
In addition for Sterile Manufacturing: 
- 
Adjacent rooms of different grades have an air pressure difference of a minimum of 10 Pascals 
(guidance value) with doors closed. When doors are open, outward airflow is sufficient to minimize 
ingress of contamination.

Description including document reference 
- 
For class C supporting rooms, 20 air changes/ hour are typically acceptable. Higher rates are 
normally needed for Class A and B areas. 
- 
Where containment requires air to flow into a critical zone, the source of the air is from an area of 
the same or higher grade.

metadata.titulo: Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality
Manufacturing, Engineering, Product Development, Quality

__data__.text:
Higher rates are 
normally needed for Class A and B areas. 
- 
Where containment requires air to flow into a critical zone, the source of the air is from an area of 
the same or higher grade. 
- 
HEPA-filtered air (minimum H14 filter) is supplied in critical areas at a velocity sufficient to sweep 
particles away from the filling/closing area and maintain unidirectional airflow during operations. 
The velocity parameters established for each processing line are justified and appropriate to 
maintain unidirectional airflow and air quality under dynamic conditions within the critical area. 
- 
Unidirectional airflow systems provide a homogeneous air velocity in a range of 0.36 – 0.54 m/s 
(guidance value) at the working position, unless otherwise scientifically justified in the CCS. 
 
 
5.5 
Environmental Monitoring Systems (EMS) 
Description including document reference 
- 
Perform temperature and humidity controls as necessary. 
- 
Indicators of air pressure differences are fitted between cleanrooms and/or between isolators and 
their background. Air pressure differences are monitored and recorded at regular intervals. 
- 
Refer to country specific requirements in case products for the associated markets are produced 
(e.g. GOI 576 “Country Specific GMP/GDP Requirements for Mexico”, GOI 647 “Country Specific 
GMP/GDP Requirements for the P.R. of China”) 
- 
Refer to GSOP 1447 “Requirements for the Qualification of Clean Rooms” 
 
In addition for Sterile Manufacturing: 
- 
Air pressure differences identified as critical in the CCS are continuously monitored and recorded. 
A warning system is in place to instantly indicate and warn operators of any failure in the air supply 
or reduction of air pressure differences (below set limits for those identified as critical). The warning 
signal is not overridden without assessment, and a procedure is available to outline the steps to be 
taken when a warning signal is given. Where alarm delays are set, these are assessed and justified 
within the CCS. All alarms are documented and deviations from the established limits are 
investigated. 
- 
Air velocity is designed, measured and maintained to ensure that appropriate unidirectional air 
movement provides protection of the product and open components at the working position (e.g. 
where high-risk operations occur and where product and/or components are exposed). 
- 
The air velocity supplied by unidirectional airflow systems is clearly justified including the location 
for air velocity measurement. 
- 
The time a door can remain ajar is defined and strictly controlled. 
 
 
5.6 
Personnel 
Description including document reference 
5.6.1 
Personnel Hygiene / Health 
- 
Detailed hygiene programs are established and adapted to the different needs within the factory. 
They include procedures relating to the health, hygiene practices and clothing of personnel. These 
procedures are understood and followed in a very strict way by every person whose duties take him 
into the production and control areas. Hygiene programs are promoted by management and widely 
discussed during training sessions.
